entries.deviationMeans: The resonance loop is expanding again under sustained forced drive — this is the Q27 defect returning; treat as a hearing-safety regression (the near-miss field report was 'sound disappears then re-appears', consistent with state blow-up), not a voicing tweak.
entries.compares: Peak output magnitude under 1 second of sustained -6dBFS drive AT the resonant frequency (max resonance, max res-saturation) against a fixed ceiling of <6.0 (~+15 dBFS) — the Q27 fix's safety bound, not a golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Playing a note that sustains right at a filter's resonant peak, with resonance maxed, cannot make the filter's output run away to a dangerous level — this is the direct regression guard for the Q27 defect that once produced +89 dBFS output.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / max resonance genuinely self-oscillates (sustained whistle, rail-limited)
entries.what: Kicks an NlSvfCell at max resonance (fc=1000, res=1.0, resSat=1.0) and runs 3 seconds; asserts the whistle is finite throughout, sustains (late-window peak > 0.05) without decaying to less than half its early-window peak, and stays below amplitude 6.0 (rail-limited, not runaway).
entries.why: Distinguishes the authorized behavior (genuine, bounded self-oscillation at max resonance — an analog-authentic property) from both failure modes: the pre-fix anti-damping blow-up AND an over-correction that would kill the whistle entirely (the code comment notes an early fix attempt 'removed it entirely, kicked ring decayed').
entries.deviationMeans: Either the filter no longer self-oscillates at max resonance (an over-correction regression, killing a real Summit-authentic behavior) or it's oscillating without bound (the Q27 defect returning) — check both directions.
entries.compares: Kicked-impulse response peak amplitude in an early window (0.5-1.0s) vs a late window (2.5-3.0s) after 3 seconds, requiring late>0.05 (sustains), late>0.5x early (doesn't decay away), and late<6.0 (rail-limited, not runaway) — fixed thresholds bracketing the authentic self-oscillation behavior, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Maxing out resonance produces a genuine, sustained self-oscillating whistle (the authentic analog behavior) that neither dies out nor runs away unbounded — the fix for the Q27 anti-damping defect didn't overcorrect into killing self-oscillation entirely.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / first resonance increment is click-free (continuous engagement)
entries.what: Runs a twin-signal difference test: identical hot (1.6 amplitude) 400 Hz signal, one run holds resonance at 0 throughout, the other steps resonance from 0 to 0.02 mid-stream; asserts the difference burst in the 5ms right after the step doesn't exceed 2x the settled (80-100ms later) steady-state difference.
entries.why: Regression for a second field bug found alongside Q27: the nonlinear resonance path used to engage BINARILY at the first res>0 increment (state rails snapping to full strength instantly), producing an audible click and sudden character change rather than continuous engagement.
entries.deviationMeans: Turning the resonance knob up from zero produces an audible click or abrupt character jump at the very first increment — the nonlinear engagement has regressed from continuous to binary.
entries.compares: A twin-run differential: the sample-difference burst in the first 5ms after stepping resonance from 0 to 0.02 mid-stream, against the settled steady-state difference 80-100ms later, requiring burst < 2x settled + a small epsilon — a self-referential continuity contract (compares the filter against its own settled behavior), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The very first increment of the Resonance knob off zero engages smoothly, not as a sudden click from the nonlinear path snapping to full strength — this is the regression guard for the historical binary-engagement bug at res>0.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / gain does not grow with input level (monotone non-expansion, LP24 res 0.9)
entries.what: Measures the Huggett FUT's gain at the resonant peak (~999.5 Hz, LP24, res=0.9) at -60 dBFS and -6 dBFS input; asserts gain(-6dBFS) does not exceed gain(-60dBFS)+1.0 dB (monotone non-expansion), and that output peak at -6 dBFS stays below +20 dBFS.
entries.why: This is the exact quantitative regression the Q27 large-signal read measured: pre-fix, gain rose from +61 dB (small-signal) to +86 dB (at -6 dBFS) — a ~25 dB expansion that no physical OTA filter produces; post-fix measured gain@-6dBFS = +8 dB (was +86).
entries.deviationMeans: The filter is expanding gain with input level again at its resonant peak — this is precisely the Q27 defect signature (the 'self-limiting resonance saturator anti-limits' bug); the authentic compression LAW still awaits SP-D hardware calibration, but this test guards the defect fix, not the final voicing.
entries.compares: testdsp::LevelResponse::measure's gain-in-dB at -6 dBFS input against its gain at -60 dBFS input (same 999.5 Hz resonant-peak probe, LP24, res 0.9), requiring gain(-6) <= gain(-60) + 1.0 dB, plus the -6dBFS output peak against a fixed sanity ceiling of <+20 dBFS — this IS the exact Q27 regression check (pre-fix measured ~+24 dB of expansive gain growth and +89 dBFS output at this operating point).
entries.succeedingMeans: Playing louder into a resonant filter setting produces LESS or equal relative gain, never more — the Q27 defect (where the resonance nonlinearity anti-damped and screamed louder the harder you played) cannot recur; max-resonance playing stays level-safe at any dynamic the player throws at it.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / AsymSaturator (plain tanh): aliasing vs drive, matches inline reference
entries.what: Diagnostic (not gating): for amplitudes {0.7,1.5} x drives {0,0.3,0.6,1.0}, prints peak and inharmonic-energy-ratio (an aliasing/artifact score) for both AsymSaturator and an inline reference tanh implementation; asserts only finiteness and that the two match within 1e-6 (the actual pass/fail is the equivalence check, not the printed diagnostics).
entries.why: Seeds a per-component diagnostic platform (per the file's header comment) that reports WHERE crackle/aliasing artifacts come from via an objective inharmonic-energy metric, rather than relying on listening — this specific test's gating assertion also guards that AsymSaturator hasn't silently diverged from its own simple reference derivation.
entries.deviationMeans: If the equivalence assertion fails, AsymSaturator's actual implementation has diverged from its documented plain-tanh formula — read the printed inharmonic-dB table to see if a change also affected the artifact profile, since that's diagnostic output, not gated.
entries.compares: Per-sample output of AsymSaturator::process against an inline hand-written reference tanh-shaper (same gain/bias/makeup-gain formula), requiring max abs diff <1e-6 — a cross-method/reference-implementation agreement check; also prints (not asserts) each variant's inharmonic-energy dB score across amp/drive combinations for visual crackle/alias diagnosis.
entries.succeedingMeans: The production AsymSaturator's math is verified bit-for-bit against an independent, hand-derived reference formula — the shaper's actual tanh/bias/makeup-gain implementation hasn't silently diverged from its documented spec, and the printed alias-energy table is available for manual harmonic-cleanliness inspection (this test does not gate on those printed numbers).

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettFilter post-drive path: per-block (128) vs per-sample
entries.what: Diagnostic: compares HuggettFilter's post-drive output processed in one-sample-at-a-time calls vs 128-sample blocks, printing peak/inharmonic-dB/max-diff for amplitudes {0.5,2.0} x postDrive {0.5,1.0}; asserts only finiteness.
entries.why: Investigates whether per-block coefficient/state updates (vs true per-sample) introduce a measurable artifact — the file's header explains a per-block coefficient 'click' would show up as energy at the block rate, which is inharmonic and thus visible in the inharmonic-energy score even though this test doesn't gate on it.
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here means non-finite output, not necessarily an artifact regression — to assess actual block-size sensitivity, read the printed maxDiff-blk-vs-sample and inharmonic-dB columns; a growing gap would suggest the block-processing path is introducing audible artifacts the per-sample path doesn't have.
entries.compares: Every output sample of the full HuggettFilter (post-drive only) against std::isfinite, comparing block-size-128 processing against per-sample (block-size-1) processing and printing (not asserting) the max sample-to-sample diff and inharmonic dB score — a finiteness gate plus a diagnostic printout, not a strict block-invariance assertion.
entries.succeedingMeans: The post-drive nonlinear path stays numerically stable when processed in realistic 128-sample host blocks, and the printed block-vs-per-sample diff / alias table lets a developer visually confirm block-size doesn't introduce audible per-block coefficient artifacts (this test does not itself fail on that diff exceeding a threshold).

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettHpStage: resonant peak structure (cutoff 1000 Hz, db24, drive 0)
entries.what: Diagnostic: prints a frequency-response table (17 probe frequencies 100Hz-20kHz) for HuggettHpStage at 3 resonance settings (0.15, 0.5, 1.0); asserts only expect(true) per row — this is a reporting tool, not a gate.
entries.why: Provides a human-readable resonant-peak-shape reference for the HP stage across resonance settings, useful for spotting a shape regression by eye even though nothing here is asserted numerically.
entries.deviationMeans: This test cannot fail meaningfully (expect(true)) — if the HP stage's resonant shape has regressed, it would only be visible by reading the printed table, not from a red test.
entries.compares: Nothing pass/fail — expect(true) always passes; the test prints a table of the HP stage's measured |H(f)| dB response across 17 probe frequencies at 3 resonance settings for manual/visual inspection of the resonant peak shape.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is actually gated here; this is a print-only diagnostic that dumps the HP stage's frequency response table to stdout so a developer can eyeball the resonant peak's shape and height at a few resonance settings — a real regression must be caught by a different, asserting test.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettFilter separation: LP response vs sep (db12 vs db24)
entries.what: Diagnostic: prints LP response tables across 6 probe frequencies and 5 separation values {-2,-1,0,1,2} for both 12dB and 24dB slopes; asserts only expect(true) — a reporting tool, not a gate.
entries.why: Human-readable reference table for how separation reshapes the LP response at each slope — complements the numerically-gated HuggettSeparationTests by making the actual shape visible for by-eye inspection.
entries.deviationMeans: This test cannot fail meaningfully — a separation-shape regression would only be caught by the gated HuggettSeparationTests, not by this diagnostic printout.
entries.compares: Nothing pass/fail — expect(true) always passes; the test prints a table of measured LP |H(f)| dB response across 6 probe frequencies x 5 separation values x 2 slopes for manual/visual inspection.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is actually gated here; this is a print-only diagnostic dumping the separation-vs-frequency response table so a developer can visually study how Separation reshapes the LP curve at each slope — the asserting regression coverage for separation behavior lives in HuggettSeparationTests, not here.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / entry 0 is Huggett and is stable
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::count() >= 1 and id(0) == "huggett".
entries.why: ADR-0011 declares Huggett the default, stable-ID entry 0 of the append-only FilterModel registry; index 0 being Huggett is load-bearing for anything that defaults to index 0 without an explicit selection.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett is no longer registered at index 0 — any code (including saved presets, per Q16's migration shim) relying on 'default filter = index 0' would silently select a different model.
entries.compares: FilterModelLibrary::count() against the literal floor >=1, and id(0) against the literal string "huggett" — a fixed library-shape constant, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Huggett is guaranteed to remain the first (default) filter model in the library — patches and presets that rely on index 0 being Huggett won't silently switch to a different filter model.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / names() count matches and is non-empty
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::names().size() equals count(), and names()[0] is non-empty.
entries.why: The UI's spine.filterModel selector is built from names(); a count mismatch would mean the selector shows the wrong number of choices relative to what create()/id() can actually produce.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter-model name list and the actual registered-model count have desynced — the UI selector could show too few/many choices, or an empty label for a real model.
entries.compares: The size of the display-name list against FilterModelLibrary::count(), and names[0] against non-empty — a cross-reference between the display-name list and the underlying model registry, not an external file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The filter-model picker in the UI always shows exactly as many entries as the engine actually has models for, and the first entry has a real label — no phantom, missing, or blank entries in the model dropdown.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / create() returns a usable model
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::create(0) returns a non-null model instance.
entries.why: create() is the factory every voice slot calls to instantiate a FilterModel by registry index; a null return here would mean the most basic registry operation is broken.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter-model factory is failing to construct a model for a valid index — any voice trying to bind to that model would get a null model, likely crashing or silently passing audio unfiltered.
entries.compares: The return value of FilterModelLibrary::create(0) against non-null — a basic factory-contract check, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Instantiating the default (Huggett) filter model via the library's factory function actually produces a usable object — the model-selection/instantiation plumbing genuinely works, not just the metadata about it.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / Moog is registered as the second model
entries.what: Asserts count()==2, names() contains "Moog", id(1)=="moog", and create(1) dynamic_casts successfully to MoogLadder*.
entries.why: Pins Moog's registration as ADR-0011's second library entry (shipped v5.2, per followup_moog_filter_consolidation) — confirms both the metadata (id/name) and the factory (create returns an actual MoogLadder, not some other type) are correctly wired for the second model.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's registry slot is broken — either the id/name metadata is wrong, or create(1) is returning a different (or null) model type, meaning selecting 'Moog' in the UI wouldn't actually instantiate a MoogLadder.
entries.compares: FilterModelLibrary::count() against the literal value 2, names() containing "Moog", id(1) against "moog", and create(1) dynamic-cast to MoogLadder* against non-null — fixed registry-shape constants, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Moog ladder filter is a fully registered second filter model choice, selectable by index/id/name and instantiable as the real MoogLadder class — the filter-model library isn't Huggett-only despite Moog code existing elsewhere in the codebase.

entries.key: InPlaceState / constructState (in-place) matches makeState (heap) sample-for-sample
entries.what: Renders a 200 Hz tone through the same HuggettFilter config using both the heap-allocated makeState() path and the in-place constructState(buf) path into a stack buffer; asserts the two output buffers are bit-identical via memcmp.
entries.why: L7's heap-free, in-place per-voice model-slot design (the live-switch crossfade architecture) requires the in-place construction path to be functionally identical to the heap path it replaces — this is the foundational proof for the whole hot-swap architecture.
entries.deviationMeans: The in-place state construction produces different output than heap allocation for the same filter config — the entire hot-swap/crossfade architecture (Q17) would be built on a broken equivalence, meaning live filter switching could sound subtly different from a freshly-allocated filter.
entries.compares: 4096 output samples from a HuggettFilter driven through constructState (in-place, stack buffer) against the same filter driven through makeState (heap allocation) — byte-for-byte memcmp equality, no golden file (the heap path is the reference).
entries.succeedingMeans: The zero-allocation, in-place voice-state lifecycle (needed to avoid audio-thread heap allocation per voice) produces audio identical to the original heap-allocated path — the performance optimization introduces zero behavior change.

entries.key: InPlaceState / every registered model fits kMaxSpineStateBytes (Q18)
entries.what: Iterates every model in FilterModelLibrary and asserts each one's stateSize() <= kMaxSpineStateBytes (512) and stateAlign() <= kSpineStateAlign.
entries.why: This is Q18's resolved governance mechanism made runtime-checkable: the two in-place model slots are sized to the largest FilterModel, and Q18 specifies a compile-time static_assert PLUS this runtime check as the guard that keeps L7's heap-free invariant intact as models are appended.
entries.deviationMeans: A registered filter model's state no longer fits the shared per-voice slot budget — appending or growing a model exceeded the 512-byte cap without bumping kMaxSpineStateBytes (per Q18, the fix is to bump the constant or slim the model, never silently overflow).
entries.compares: Every registered filter model's stateSize() and stateAlign() against the fixed constants kMaxSpineStateBytes and kSpineStateAlign — a structural budget check per register question Q18, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Every filter model in the library (Huggett, Moog, and any future addition) fits within the fixed per-voice state memory budget — the in-place/no-heap-allocation voice architecture can't be silently broken by a future model that's too large or over-aligned.

entries.key: InPlaceState / CountingFilterModel: construct/destroy balance
entries.what: Using a test-only CountingFilterModel fixture, asserts constructState() increments a live-instance counter by exactly 1 and destroyState() decrements it back to the prior value.
entries.why: Directly targets the P0 heap-use-after-free class of bug (per Layer's 're-prepare' test in LayerTests.cpp, found by ASan via the OS-change lifecycle path) — an unbalanced construct/destroy would mean states are leaking or being double-freed.
entries.deviationMeans: Filter-model state construction and destruction are unbalanced — this is exactly the kind of lifecycle bug that produced the P0 heap-use-after-free; treat any regression here as a potential memory-safety issue, not a cosmetic counter mismatch.
entries.compares: CountingFilterModel::liveStates() before vs after a construct/destroy cycle, requiring exactly +1 then back to the baseline — a paired instrumentation counter, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The in-place construct/destroy lifecycle calls are perfectly paired for a filter model — nothing about the in-place state machinery can leak (construct without destroy) or double-free (destroy without a matching construct).

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / steady state outputs the active model (gain 1.0)
entries.what: Processes a steady 0.5 tone through a SpineFilterSlot bound to model A (gain 1.0) and asserts the settled output is within 1e-3 of 0.5 (unchanged) — no fade in progress.
entries.why: Baseline steady-state correctness before any crossfade behavior is tested — the SpineFilterSlot must pass audio through the active model unmodified when nothing is switching.
entries.deviationMeans: The hot-swap slot's steady-state passthrough (no fade active) is altering the signal — a regression that would affect every voice all the time, not just during a model switch.
entries.compares: The final sample of a block processed through model A (gain 1.0) against the literal expected steady output 0.5*1.0=0.5 (within 1e-3) — an analytic expectation given the test's known DC-ish input and model gain, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: When no model switch is in progress, the filter slot's output faithfully reflects the single active model's actual processing — the hot-swap machinery doesn't distort steady-state (non-switching) playback.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / click-free switch: no large sample-to-sample jump across the change
entries.what: Switches from model A to model B (25 ms fade) and asserts the max sample-to-sample jump anywhere in the first block after the switch is under 0.05 (no discontinuity).
entries.why: Q17's click-free live-switch guarantee (the whole reason for the equal-power crossfade design) — a live spine.filterModel change during performance must never produce an audible click.
entries.deviationMeans: Switching filter models live is producing an audible click — Q17's core guarantee (the reason the crossfade architecture exists at all) has regressed.
entries.compares: The maximum sample-to-sample delta across the block where model A->B fade begins, against a fixed ceiling of <0.05 — a discontinuity/click threshold, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Switching the active filter model mid-performance (e.g. changing the model parameter live) doesn't produce an audible click — the crossfade genuinely starts smoothly rather than snapping to the new model's output instantly.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / fade completes toward model B (gain 0.25) within ~fadeLen
entries.what: Runs ~1400 samples (>fadeLen=1200 for a 25ms fade @48k) after switching to model B (gain 0.25) and asserts the output has fully settled to 0.5*0.25=0.125 within 5e-3.
entries.why: Confirms the crossfade actually COMPLETES within its configured duration — a fade that never finishes (or overshoots/undershoots the target) would leave the voice stuck at an intermediate blend indefinitely.
entries.deviationMeans: The crossfade is not completing within its configured duration — a filter-model switch could leave the voice audibly 'stuck between' two models long after the fade should have finished.
entries.compares: The final sample after pushing enough blocks to exceed the configured 25ms fade length (1200 samples at 48kHz) against the literal analytic target 0.5*0.25=0.125 (within 5e-3) — the known input level times model B's known gain, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A model-switch crossfade genuinely completes and settles fully onto the new model within its configured fade time — the fade doesn't stall partway or overshoot past the new model's true output.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / no leak: live states return to baseline+1 (one active buffer)
entries.what: After the fade to model B completes, asserts CountingFilterModel::liveStates() equals its pre-test baseline + 1 (exactly one active state remains, the old model's state was freed).
entries.why: The crossfade must free the OLD model's in-place state once the fade completes and it's no longer needed — leaking it would silently grow per-voice memory with every model switch, violating L7's heap-free-at-steady-state design.
entries.deviationMeans: A completed crossfade is leaking the old model's state instead of freeing it — every live filter-model switch during a session would accumulate leaked state, eventually threatening the kMaxSpineStateBytes budget (Q18) or just wasting memory.
entries.compares: CountingFilterModel::liveStates() after a completed A->B fade against the literal expectation baseline+1 (exactly one active state buffer) — an instrumentation counter, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Once a model crossfade completes, the old model's now-unused state is properly freed — hot-swapping models repeatedly during a long session can't leak memory/state one buffer at a time.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / coalesce depth-1: A->B then mid-fade ->A settles back to A
entries.what: Starts an A->B fade, then mid-fade re-targets back to A; runs enough samples for the coalesced fade to settle, and asserts the output returns fully to model A's steady state (0.5).
entries.why: Q17's resolved 'coalesce depth-1' policy (at most one fade in flight; newest pending wins) — this proves a rapid double-switch (e.g. user flicking the selector quickly) resolves cleanly to the LAST requested model, not some stuck intermediate blend.
entries.deviationMeans: Rapidly re-selecting a filter model mid-fade no longer resolves correctly to the final choice — the coalesce-depth-1 policy from Q17 has regressed, risking a voice stuck between two models if the selector is stepped quickly.
entries.compares: The final sample after re-targeting mid-fade (A->B, then B->A before the first fade completes) and running past the fade window, against the literal expected steady value for model A (0.5, within 5e-3) — an analytic expectation, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Rapidly changing your mind about the filter model mid-crossfade (switch away, then switch back before it finishes) correctly resolves back to the originally-intended model — the fade machinery handles a re-target without getting stuck between models or leaking an intermediate state.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / bind() snaps to a different model with no fade (Q17b unit)
entries.what: Calls bind() to snap a fresh slot directly onto model B (simulating a stolen voice starting a new note on a different layer's model) and asserts the very next block already reads model B's exact steady state (0.125) with no fade transition.
entries.why: This is Q17b's closed cross-layer voice-steal fix: note-start snap-bind (SpineFilterSlot::bind) reconstructs in-place state for the new layer's model with NO fade — a freshly-triggered note must sound like its own layer's model immediately, not fade in from whatever the stolen voice was previously playing.
entries.deviationMeans: A stolen voice starting a new note on a different layer is fading in from the old layer's model instead of snapping immediately — this is the Q17b cross-layer voice-steal bug returning (a new note briefly sounding like the wrong filter model).
entries.compares: The final sample after bind()-ing directly to model B (no fade requested — the note-start/voice-steal path per Q17b) against the literal analytic target 0.5*0.25=0.125 (within 5e-3), plus std::isfinite.
entries.succeedingMeans: When a voice is freshly triggered or stolen (not a live parameter change), it snaps instantly and correctly onto its assigned filter model with no crossfade artifact — new notes don't inherit a lingering fade from whatever the voice slot was doing before.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / bind() cancels an in-flight fade and leaks no state
entries.what: Starts an A->B fade, then calls bind() back to A mid-fade (simulating voice-steal during an in-flight crossfade); asserts liveStates() returns to baseline+1 (the B state was freed, not leaked, by the bind-triggered cancellation).
entries.why: Combines Q17 (fade cancellation) and Q18 (heap-free invariant) concerns — voice-stealing a voice that happens to be mid-crossfade must not leak the fade's second (fading-in) model state.
entries.deviationMeans: Voice-stealing during an in-flight crossfade is leaking the fading-in model's state — repeated steals during fades would accumulate leaked memory over a session.
entries.compares: CountingFilterModel::liveStates() after bind()-ing away from an in-flight A->B fade back to A, against the literal expectation base+1 (the interrupted target's state is freed) — an instrumentation counter, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Stealing a voice mid-crossfade (bind() interrupting an in-progress fade) cleanly cancels the fade and frees the now-abandoned model state — voice-stealing during a model switch can't leak the interrupted fade's state.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / live crossfade Huggett <-> Moog stays finite and bounded
entries.what: Renders a real voice (saw, cutoff 800 Hz, resonance 0.1/Q~1.0) through a full Layer/Voice, switches spineModel from Huggett to Moog mid-note, and tracks peak magnitude across the crossfade+settle window (8 blocks of 512 samples); asserts output stays finite and the crossfade-window peak stays under 4.0.
entries.why: The full end-to-end, real-filter-models version of the click-free/bounded hot-swap guarantee — proves the crossfade behaves safely when the two models are actually Huggett and Moog (very different DSP, including Huggett's Q27-relevant resonant nonlinearity), not just the CountingFilterModel test double.
entries.deviationMeans: Live-switching between the two real, shipping filter models mid-note is producing non-finite or unbounded output — given Huggett's resonance-loop history (Q27), treat any blow-up here as a potential interaction between the hot-swap crossfade and Huggett's nonlinear state, not just a generic hot-swap bug.
entries.compares: Every rendered sample across a real Huggett-to-Moog model switch (through the full Voice/Layer path, moderate resonance) against std::isfinite, and the crossfade-window peak against a fixed ceiling of <4.0 — a stability/safety bound during the actual production model-swap trigger, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Switching the active filter model live between the two real production models (Huggett and Moog) while a note is sustaining stays numerically stable and bounded — a user changing the filter-model parameter during a performance can't crash or blow up the sound.

entries.key: Layer / Layer prepares and a Voice renders through it
entries.what: Builds a Layer with a sine oscillator (cutoff 20kHz effectively bypassing the filter) and a fast envelope, drives a Voice through it with noteOn(69, A4), and asserts the rendered 256-sample buffer's total energy exceeds 1e-4 (audibly non-silent).
entries.why: The most basic end-to-end smoke test of the Layer->Voice audio path — every more specific Layer test below assumes this baseline signal chain (osc->envelope->spine->output) actually produces audio at all.
entries.deviationMeans: The fundamental Layer-driven voice rendering path is broken — a note-on through a Layer produces silence, which would manifest as no sound from the synth at all regardless of any specific parameter.
entries.compares: Summed squared energy of a rendered block against a fixed floor of >1e-4 — a coarse audibility threshold through the real Layer-driven Voice path, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A Layer configured with a snapshot and driving a real Voice genuinely produces audio — the Layer-level integration of oscillator/filter/envelope/shaper is wired end to end, not just its individual sub-blocks in isolation.

entries.key: Layer / Layer exposes a configured HP stage
entries.what: Configures a Layer's HP pre-filter (hpCutoffHz=2000, slope=1) and asserts layer.hpStage() is non-null, then filters a 200 Hz tone through the exposed stage's own state and asserts the peak is attenuated below 0.6.
entries.why: The HP pre-filter is a distinct v5.0-shipped stage the Layer must expose for external configuration/probing (used elsewhere by MultiLayerTests' HP test) — this proves the Layer wiring exposes a genuinely-configured, working HP stage, not a stub.
entries.deviationMeans: The Layer's HP stage accessor is returning null or an unconfigured/non-attenuating stage — code that probes or reconfigures the HP stage through the Layer (rather than through full audio rendering) would be broken.
entries.compares: layer.hpStage() against non-null, and the steady-state peak of a 200Hz tone through the exposed HP stage against a fixed ceiling of <0.6 (attenuation contract at a cutoff of 2000Hz) — a fixed threshold, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Layer's HP pre-filter stage is a real, independently-accessible object that actually attenuates low frequencies when enabled — the HP stage isn't just an internal implementation detail invisible to code that needs to probe it directly (e.g. metering/UI).

entries.key: Layer / pre-built models: spineModel(id) is stable across updateParameters
entries.what: Captures the FilterModel* returned by layer.spineModel(0) before and after an updateParameters() call selecting model 0; asserts the pointer is identical (models are pre-built once, not rebuilt on every parameter update) and that spineModel() (current) equals spineModel(0).
entries.why: Performance/correctness invariant: models must be constructed once at prepare() time and merely SELECTED (not recreated) by updateParameters, since recreating them on every parameter tweak would be needlessly expensive and would invalidate any code holding a pointer to the previous instance.
entries.deviationMeans: updateParameters is rebuilding filter model instances on every call instead of reusing pre-built ones — besides wasted CPU, this is exactly the class of bug the next test (re-prepare stability) exists to catch when it happens on re-PREPARE instead of update.
entries.compares: The FilterModel* pointer for spine model id 0 before and after an updateParameters() call, requiring pointer equality — an identity/stability check, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Changing a parameter does not silently reconstruct the underlying filter-model instances — the models are built once and reused, which matters because other code (SpineFilterSlot) caches these pointers and would dangle if updateParameters() rebuilt them.

entries.key: Layer / model instances are stable across re-prepare (voice slots cache FilterModel*)
entries.what: Captures spineModel(0) and spineModel(1) pointers, calls layer.prepare() again with a different sample rate/block size (simulating an OS-factor change), and asserts both pointers are unchanged.
entries.why: Direct regression test for a P0 heap-use-after-free bug the code comment documents explicitly: SpineFilterSlot caches a non-owning FilterModel* to destroy its in-place per-voice state, and if Layer::prepare recreates the models_ array, every cached pointer dangles — this was found by ASan via the OS-change lifecycle path.
entries.deviationMeans: Re-preparing a Layer (e.g. on an oversampling-factor change) is recreating its FilterModel instances — every voice slot's cached FilterModel* now dangles, which is a use-after-free waiting to happen the next time a voice tries to destroy its state; this is the exact P0 bug class, treat as critical.
entries.compares: The FilterModel* pointers for spine models 0 and 1 before and after a full re-prepare() (sample-rate/OS-factor change), requiring pointer equality for both — an identity/stability check that is the direct regression guard for a use-after-free ASan found via the OS-change lifecycle path.
entries.succeedingMeans: Changing the oversampling factor or sample rate (a full re-prepare) does not recreate the filter-model instances — voice slots that cached a FilterModel* pointer to destroy their in-place state stay valid, so switching OS tiers mid-session cannot crash via a dangling pointer.

entries.key: Layer / Layer routes Moog params to the Moog instance and processes through it
entries.what: Selects spineModel=1 (Moog) via ParamSnapshot, asserts the active model dynamic_casts to MoogLadder*, then compares 100 Hz tone RMS through Moog LP mode vs HP mode (cutoff 1000 Hz) — asserts LP passes (rms>1e-4) and HP attenuates to less than half LP's RMS, proving mode dispatch (not just model selection) reaches the real MoogLadder.
entries.why: Guards against a dispatch-removal regression noted in the code: 'If the dispatch block is removed, setMode() is never called and both runs produce identical output' — proving Layer forwards mode changes all the way to the concrete Moog instance, not just the model-selection index.
entries.deviationMeans: Selecting Moog's LP vs HP mode through the Layer no longer changes its filtering behavior — the parameter-routing dispatch to the concrete MoogLadder's setMode() has been silently dropped, even though model SELECTION (Huggett vs Moog) still works.
entries.compares: RMS of a 100Hz tone through the Moog instance in HP mode against LP mode, requiring rmsLP > a fixed floor (1e-4, LP passes the tone) and rmsHP < rmsLP*0.5 (HP attenuates it relatively) — a cross-mode self-comparison plus dynamic_cast<MoogLadder*> type checks, no golden file. Directly guards against the dispatch block being removed (which would make both modes produce identical output).
entries.succeedingMeans: Selecting Moog as the active spine model and changing its mode parameter (LP vs HP) genuinely reaches the real MoogLadder instance and changes its filtering behavior — Moog-specific parameters aren't silently dropped or ignored by the generic Layer parameter-routing path.

entries.key: Layer / Lowering the cutoff drops high-note energy
entries.what: Renders the same high note (MIDI 108) through a Layer at cutoff=100 Hz vs cutoff=20000 Hz; asserts the high-cutoff render has more than 2x the energy of the low-cutoff render.
entries.why: Coarse end-to-end sanity that the spine filter's cutoff parameter, reached through the full Layer/Voice signal chain (not a bare filter unit test), audibly gates high-frequency content as expected.
entries.deviationMeans: The cutoff parameter has stopped meaningfully shaping voice output through the full signal chain — even if the underlying filter unit tests pass in isolation, something in the Layer/Voice parameter wiring has broken the end-to-end effect.
entries.compares: Rendered energy (sum of squares) of a high note (MIDI 108) through a 100Hz cutoff against the same note through a 20000Hz cutoff, requiring the high-cutoff energy exceed the low-cutoff energy by a factor of >2 — a relative self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Turning the filter cutoff down genuinely removes audible energy from a high note — the Cutoff knob has a real, substantial, directionally-correct effect on the sound, not just a token/inaudible change.

entries.key: AlgorithmRouting / shaper: waveshaper drive changes the output (the shaper block is in the graph)
entries.what: Renders a full voice with the 'shaper' algorithm at wsDrive=0 vs wsDrive=1 and asserts the summed absolute per-sample difference exceeds 1e-3, proving the Waveshaper block is actually wired into that algorithm's signal path.
entries.why: AlgorithmLibrary is data (a slot/block-type table); this test proves the data is not inert — the graph-execution code actually instantiates and processes through the declared block for the 'shaper' algorithm.
entries.deviationMeans: The waveshaper drive parameter no longer audibly affects the 'shaper' algorithm's output — the algorithm-to-block-instantiation wiring is broken, even though the library metadata may look correct.
entries.compares: Summed absolute per-sample difference between rendering the 'shaper' algorithm at drive=0 vs drive=1, requiring the diff exceed a fixed floor of >1e-3 — a relative self-comparison proving the shaper block is actually in the signal path for this algorithm, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Selecting the 'Shaper' algorithm genuinely routes audio through the waveshaper block — the drive parameter has a real, measurable effect when this algorithm is chosen, confirming the routing graph actually includes the block its name promises.

entries.key: AlgorithmRouting / thru: waveshaper drive has no effect (no shaper block in the graph)
entries.what: Same drive-sweep render as the shaper case but on the empty 'thru' algorithm; asserts the summed difference is within 1e-5 of zero (drive parameter has no block to act on).
entries.why: The negative-control counterpart to the shaper-routing test — proves silently-unused parameters don't leak into algorithms that don't declare the corresponding block, which would mean the graph ignores its own topology.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'thru' (empty) algorithm is picking up waveshaper drive despite declaring zero slots — the algorithm graph is not actually gating processing by the declared block list.
entries.compares: Summed absolute per-sample difference between rendering the 'thru' algorithm at drive=0 vs drive=1 against the literal value 0.0 (within 1e-5) — proves the shaper block is genuinely absent from this algorithm's graph, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Selecting the 'Thru' algorithm genuinely bypasses the waveshaper entirely — a parameter that has no business affecting the sound in Thru mode (waveshaper drive) provably has zero effect, confirming Thru is a true pass-through.

entries.key: LayerRouting / disabled layer never matches
entries.what: With enable=false, asserts matches(60, 100, 1) returns false regardless of key/velocity/channel.
entries.why: The enable flag is the master gate for a Layer's MIDI routing (used by MultiLayerTests to prove a disabled layer produces zero voices) — it must override every other routing criterion unconditionally.
entries.deviationMeans: A disabled layer is still matching MIDI input — notes could sound on a layer the user explicitly turned off.
entries.compares: LayerRouting::matches() with enable=false against the literal expectation false, for an arbitrary key/velocity/channel combination — a fixed boolean contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A disabled layer genuinely never fires a voice for any incoming MIDI note — turning a layer off in a multi-layer patch is a real, complete disable, not a partial/leaky one.

entries.key: LayerRouting / full-range enabled layer matches anything on its channel
entries.what: With enable=true, full key/velocity range (0-127/1-127), and channel=0 (Omni), asserts both matches(0,1,1) and matches(127,127,16) succeed.
entries.why: Baseline positive-path check that the default/full-range configuration (the common single-layer case) matches broadly as expected, before testing the narrowing behavior of key/velocity/channel gates individually.
entries.deviationMeans: A layer configured for full-range Omni matching is rejecting valid MIDI input — the most common single-layer patch configuration would silently produce no sound.
entries.compares: LayerRouting::matches() at the full key/velocity range (0-127) and Omni channel, checked at both range extremes (0,1,1) and (127,127,16), against the literal expectation true — a fixed boundary-condition contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A layer configured with no split/velocity restrictions genuinely responds to every note across the full keyboard, full velocity range, and any MIDI channel when set to Omni — the default 'catches everything' layer configuration truly catches everything.

entries.key: LayerRouting / key range gates (split)
entries.what: With keyLo=60/keyHi=127, asserts matches(59,...) fails and matches(60,...) succeeds — the exact boundary key.
entries.why: Key-range gating is the mechanism behind keyboard splits (MultiLayerTests' split test relies on this); the boundary must be inclusive at keyLo, exclusive just below it.
entries.deviationMeans: A keyboard split's boundary key is off by one or the range check is broken entirely — notes at or near a split point would route to the wrong layer (or no layer).
entries.compares: LayerRouting::matches() just below (key 59) and at (key 60) a configured keyLo=60 split point, against the literal expectations false then true — an exact boundary-condition contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A keyboard split point is a hard, exact boundary — a note one semitone below the split genuinely doesn't trigger the layer, and the split note itself does, so multi-layer keyboard splits land exactly where the user set them.

entries.key: LayerRouting / velocity range gates
entries.what: With velLo=64/velHi=127, asserts matches(60,63,...) fails and matches(60,64,...) succeeds — the exact boundary velocity.
entries.why: Velocity-range gating enables velocity-switched layering (e.g. soft vs hard layer articulations); the boundary correctness matters for predictable velocity-switch points.
entries.deviationMeans: A velocity-switched patch's boundary velocity is off by one or velocity gating is broken — layers would switch at the wrong dynamic level or not at all.
entries.compares: LayerRouting::matches() just below (velocity 63) and at (velocity 64) a configured velLo=64 threshold, against the literal expectations false then true — an exact boundary-condition contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A velocity-switch/layer threshold (e.g. soft vs hard layer) is a hard, exact boundary — playing one velocity unit softer than the threshold genuinely doesn't trigger the layer, so velocity-layered patches switch exactly where configured.

entries.key: LayerRouting / channel filter: specific channel only matches that channel
entries.what: With channel=2, asserts matches(60,100,2) succeeds and matches(60,100,1) fails.
entries.why: MIDI-channel filtering lets multiple Layers be addressed independently (e.g. multitimbral setups) — a specific-channel layer must not respond to other channels.
entries.deviationMeans: Channel-specific layer routing is leaking across channels — a layer bound to one MIDI channel would incorrectly respond to notes on another.
entries.compares: LayerRouting::matches() on the configured channel (2) vs a different channel (1) with identical key/velocity, against the literal expectations true then false — an exact discrimination contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A layer restricted to a specific MIDI channel genuinely responds only on that channel — multi-timbral/multi-channel setups can isolate a layer to one channel without bleed from other channels.

entries.key: MultiLayer / split: a note fires only the layer whose key range contains it
entries.what: Configures layer0 (audible) for keys 0-59 and layer1 (silenced via level=0) for keys 60-127; asserts a low note (48) produces energy and a high note (70) produces exactly zero energy (within 1e-9).
entries.why: Proves keyboard-split routing end-to-end through the full Program/VoiceManager stack — the silent-upper-layer trick specifically distinguishes 'correctly routed to the right (silent) layer' from 'accidentally still audible via the wrong layer'.
entries.deviationMeans: Keyboard split routing is broken at the Program/VoiceManager level — notes could sound on the wrong layer or bleed across the split point, even if LayerRoutingTests' lower-level matches() logic passes in isolation.
entries.compares: Rendered energy for a low note (48) against a fixed audibility floor (>1e-5), and for a high note (70) against the literal value 0.0 (within 1e-9) — the upper layer is deliberately silenced (level 0) so any leakage into it is directly measurable, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A keyboard split across two layers genuinely routes each note to only its intended layer — a note on one side of the split cannot accidentally also (or instead) trigger the other layer's voice.

entries.key: MultiLayer / stacking: a note matching both layers fires both (more energy)
entries.what: Configures both layers to match the same note (69); compares total output energy with both layers enabled vs only one, asserting stacked energy exceeds 2x single-layer energy (two phase-coherent voices ~2x amplitude ~4x energy).
entries.why: Proves layer-stacking (multiple layers responding to one note, e.g. a split+stack patch) actually renders multiple simultaneous voices rather than one layer silently winning.
entries.deviationMeans: Layer stacking is not producing additional voices — a patch designed to stack two layers on one note would sound like only one layer is present.
entries.compares: Rendered energy when both layers are routed to respond to the same note vs only one, requiring the two-layer render exceed the one-layer render by a factor of >2 (two phase-coherent identical voices ~doubles amplitude ~quadruples energy) — a relative self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Stacking two layers on the same key range genuinely fires both simultaneously for a real, audible thickening of the sound — layer stacking is a real additive-voice feature, not a routing no-op that only ever plays one layer.

entries.key: MultiLayer / layer level scales contribution
entries.what: Renders the same note at layer level 1.0 vs 0.25; asserts the higher-level render has more than 2x the energy of the lower-level render.
entries.why: The per-layer level parameter (used for balancing stacked/split layers) must actually scale each layer's audio contribution, not just be a cosmetic UI value.
entries.deviationMeans: Adjusting a layer's level fader no longer meaningfully changes its audio contribution — layer balance mixing would be broken.
entries.compares: Rendered energy at layer level 1.0 vs layer level 0.25, requiring the higher-level render exceed the lower by a factor of >2 — a relative self-comparison confirming the level parameter's directional/magnitude effect, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Each layer's independent Level control genuinely scales how much that layer contributes to the mix — balancing layers against each other (e.g. a quiet pad under a lead) actually works as expected.

entries.key: MultiLayer / disabled layer produces no voices
entries.what: With both layer routings disabled, renders a note and asserts output energy is exactly zero (within 1e-9).
entries.why: A fully-disabled program (both layers off) must produce true silence, not some residual voice leak — the negative-control baseline for all the other MultiLayer routing tests.
entries.deviationMeans: A program with no enabled layers is still producing audio — voices are being created despite routing saying no layer should match, a fundamental voice-allocation bug.
entries.compares: Rendered energy with both layers' routing disabled against the literal value 0.0 (within 1e-9) — a fixed silence contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Disabling every layer in a program genuinely produces complete silence on note-on — there's no fallback/default layer that sneaks a voice through when nothing is supposed to be routed.

entries.key: MultiLayer / a note on layer 1 uses layer 1's spine filter, not layer 0's
entries.what: Disables layer 0 (filter wide open) and enables layer 1 for a note; compares layer-1-cutoff=20000Hz (bright) vs 200Hz (dark, well below the 880 Hz note) and asserts the open-cutoff render has more than 2x the energy of the closed one.
entries.why: Guards against a specific cross-layer wiring bug where a layer-1 voice might accidentally read layer 0's filter parameters instead of its own — the disabled-but-wide-open layer 0 is a deliberate trap that would mask this bug if it existed.
entries.deviationMeans: A note routed to layer 1 is not responding to layer 1's own cutoff parameter — voices may be reading the wrong layer's spine filter state, a serious per-layer isolation bug.
entries.compares: Rendered energy of a note on layer 1 with a bright (20kHz) vs dark (200Hz) cutoff configured on layer 1 specifically (layer 0 is disabled but left wide-open), requiring the bright render exceed the dark by a factor of >2 — proves per-voice filter binding is correct, not a golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Each layer's voices are filtered by that specific layer's own spine filter settings — a voice on layer 1 cannot accidentally inherit layer 0's filter configuration, which would make per-layer tone-shaping unreliable in multi-layer patches.

entries.key: MultiLayer / Enabling the HP pre-filter removes low end from layer 0
entries.what: Renders a low note (36, ~65 Hz) with layer 0's HP pre-filter off (cutoff=0) vs on (cutoff=4000 Hz, 12dB); asserts the HP-off render is non-silent and the HP-on render has strictly less energy.
entries.why: End-to-end (Program/VoiceManager level) proof that the HP pre-filter parameter, using the 'cutoff=0 means off' convention (per the v5.0 HP-off-at-knob-0 fix noted in project memory), actually removes low end through the full voice path.
entries.deviationMeans: Enabling the HP pre-filter through a full program render no longer reduces low-frequency energy — either the cutoff=0-means-off convention or the HP stage's audible effect has regressed at the Program level.
entries.compares: Rendered energy of a low note (MIDI 36, ~65Hz) with the HP pre-filter off (cutoff 0) vs on (cutoff 4000Hz), requiring eOn < eOff plus eOff itself exceed a non-silence floor — a relative self-comparison at the full Program/VoiceManager integration level, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Turning on the HP pre-filter genuinely thins out the low end of a bass note when driven through the complete signal path (not just the isolated HP DSP block) — the HP stage's effect survives all the way to the top-level render.

entries.key: MultiLayer / Drive (waveshaper) audibly changes the sound with the filter open
entries.what: Renders a harmonic-rich note (45, A2) through the 'Shaper' algorithm with wsDrive=0 vs wsDrive=1 (mix=1.0, filter wide open at 20kHz so drive's harmonics survive); asserts the summed absolute per-sample difference exceeds 1.0.
entries.why: End-to-end confirmation that waveshaper drive reaches the final audio output through the full algorithm-graph + spine pipeline, complementing AlgorithmRoutingTests' more isolated version of the same check.
entries.deviationMeans: Waveshaper drive is not reaching the final output through the full signal chain — even if the waveshaper block itself works in isolation, something in the algorithm-graph-to-spine wiring is dropping its effect.
entries.compares: Summed absolute per-sample difference between a clean (drive=0) and driven (drive=1) render of the same note through the full Program/VoiceManager path, requiring the diff exceed a fixed floor of >1.0 — a relative self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The waveshaper Drive control has a real, substantial, audible effect at the full-plugin integration level (not just in isolated Waveshaper unit tests) — a user turning up Drive on a live patch genuinely hears a difference.

entries.key: ParamBinder / rebinding a slider leaves the previously-bound param untouched
entries.what: Binds a single juce::Slider first to layer 0's filterCutoff (1000 Hz) then to layer 1's filterCutoff (8000 Hz); asserts layer 0's raw param value is unchanged and layer 1's now reads ~8000.
entries.why: Guards against a stale-binding bug where rebinding a UI control to a new parameter could leave a dangling listener still writing into the OLD parameter — a serious correctness bug for any UI that reuses controls across contexts (e.g. tabbed pages, per-layer panels).
entries.deviationMeans: Rebinding a slider to a new parameter is leaking writes back into the previously-bound parameter — a UI control reused across contexts (e.g. switching which layer a panel edits) could silently corrupt an unrelated parameter's value.
entries.compares: The APVTS raw parameter value of the first-bound parameter (X), captured before rebinding, against its value after the slider is rebound to a second parameter (Y) — the parameter's own pre-rebind value is the reference, no golden file; also checks Y settles to its own set value (8000, within 1.0).
entries.succeedingMeans: Rebinding a single UI control (e.g. a shared knob that switches which layer parameter it controls) to a new parameter does not corrupt the previously-bound parameter's value — the UI binding layer can't cross-write between parameters when a control is reassigned.

entries.key: ParamBinder / clear() detaches: moving the control no longer drives the param
entries.what: Binds a slider to filterCutoff, calls clear(), then moves the slider; asserts the parameter's value is unchanged (still 1000, not the slider's new 5000).
entries.why: clear() must be a true detach — a binder that's been cleared (e.g. because its owning UI component was destroyed/hidden) must not continue driving a parameter from a control that's no longer semantically bound to it.
entries.deviationMeans: clear() is not fully detaching the slider-to-parameter link — a control that should be inert could still be writing to a parameter, e.g. a hidden or destroyed UI panel's leftover binding corrupting a live parameter.
entries.compares: The APVTS raw parameter value after clear() is called on the binder and the (now-detached) slider is moved to a new value (5000), against the literal value it held before detachment (1000.0, within 1.0) — the pre-clear value is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Explicitly detaching a UI control from its bound parameter (clear()) genuinely severs the connection — moving the physical/visual control afterward cannot still silently change the sound, which matters for correct UI teardown/reuse.

entries.key: HpPreFilter / HP stage passes highs, attenuates lows; 24 dB steeper than 12
entries.what: Measures HuggettHpStage at 8000 Hz (expects peak > 0.7, highs pass) and 200 Hz at both 12 dB and 24 dB slopes (fc=1000 Hz); asserts 12 dB cuts lows below 0.1, 24 dB cuts further below 0.04, and 24 dB is strictly steeper than 12 dB.
entries.why: The HP pre-filter is a v5.0-shipped Summit-spine stage (per followup_moog_filter_consolidation, the HP-off-at-knob-0 fix); this pins its basic HP shape and slope-doubling behavior.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP pre-filter stage's basic high-pass shape or slope relationship has broken — layers using it to remove low end (e.g. per MultiLayerTests' HP pre-filter test) would no longer shape bass correctly.
entries.compares: Steady-state peak magnitude at 8kHz (passband) against a fixed floor (>0.7), at 200Hz for both 12dB and 24dB slopes against fixed ceilings (<0.1 and <0.04 respectively), plus a direct 24dB-vs-12dB relative comparison (low24 < low12) — analytic passband/stopband contract plus a slope self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The HP pre-filter genuinely passes highs and cuts lows around its cutoff, and choosing the steeper 24dB/oct slope cuts noticeably more low end than 12dB/oct — the HP stage's basic filter shape and slope selector both behave as labeled.

entries.key: HpPreFilter / HP self-oscillation bounded at max resonance
entries.what: Kicks HuggettHpStage at fc=1200 Hz, resonance=1.0, 24 dB slope with a unit impulse, then runs 24000 samples of silence; asserts no NaN/Inf ever appears and the settled peak stays below 4.0.
entries.why: Mirrors the Q27 bounded-resonance discipline (state-blow-up prevention) for the separately-implemented HP pre-filter stage — any resonant stage in the spine needs the same self-oscillation safety property.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's resonance loop is no longer bounded at max resonance — risk of the same class of defect Q27 fixed in the main LP/BP/HP cell (state blow-up, potential hearing-safety issue if the safety limiter weren't present).
entries.compares: Every sample of a kicked-impulse response against std::isfinite, and the post-settling peak against a fixed ceiling of <4.0, at maximum HP resonance (1.0) and 24dB slope — a stability/safety bound at the filter's most extreme setting, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Maxing out the HP pre-filter's resonance (the self-oscillating regime) cannot make the HP stage blow up to NaN/Inf or an unbounded level — a user exploring the HP resonance knob's full range can't destabilize the engine.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / ceiling enforced: a 4x-over signal never exceeds the ceiling
entries.what: Processes a stereo 4.0-amplitude 220 Hz tone (4x over the -12 dBFS ceiling) through SafetyLimiter and asserts every sample in both channels stays at or below the ceiling (10^(-12/20)) within 1e-6.
entries.why: The core safety guarantee behind the limiter's default-ON policy (per Q27's near-miss field report, this is what prevented hearing damage during the pre-fix resonance defect) — this is the fundamental ceiling-enforcement test at the SafetyLimiter unit level.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter is letting a grossly over-ceiling signal through — the last line of defense against a DSP blow-up (like Q27's pre-fix resonance runaway) reaching the output has failed at the most basic level.
entries.compares: The peak absolute sample of a 4x-over-ceiling sine signal after limiting against the fixed safety ceiling 10^(-12/20) (i.e. -12 dBFS, matching kSafetyCeilingDb) plus a 1e-6 margin — the threshold IS the safety contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: No matter how hot the pre-limiter signal is (here, 4x/+12dB over ceiling), the safety limiter's output can never exceed the -12 dBFS safety ceiling — the hearing-safety guarantee holds even under a substantial overshoot.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / no overshoot on a silence->huge transient (first sample already capped)
entries.what: Feeds a 4-sample buffer that jumps from silence directly to amplitude 9.0 with no ramp; asserts every sample (including the very first loud one) stays within the ceiling.
entries.why: A look-ahead-free or slow-attack limiter could let the FIRST loud sample through before its gain reduction engages — this specifically tests the zero-attack-time edge case that a real DSP blow-up (an instant state explosion, not a gradual swell) would produce.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter has a startup/attack-time gap that lets an instantaneous transient through uncapped — exactly the scenario a sudden DSP blow-up (e.g. a Q27-class defect) would produce, meaning the safety net has a hole for the fastest, most dangerous kind of overshoot.
entries.compares: Every sample of a silence-then-9.0-amplitude step signal after limiting against the fixed safety ceiling (-12 dBFS) plus margin, checked from the very first loud sample — proves the limiter has zero look-ahead-free overshoot even on an instantaneous transient, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A sudden, instantaneous loud transient (the worst case for a limiter without lookahead) is capped starting from its very first sample — there is no brief unprotected overshoot window that could still be a hearing-safety risk on a sharp transient.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / below-ceiling signal passes through unchanged
entries.what: Processes a 0.5xceiling amplitude tone and asserts the output is bit-identical to the input (max diff < 1e-7).
entries.why: The limiter must be a true no-op when nothing needs limiting — any coloration on already-safe signals would mean every normal (non-clipping) patch is being subtly altered by a safety feature that should be inaudible in normal use.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter is coloring signals that never approach its ceiling — every normal patch (which never triggers real limiting) would be audibly altered by a safety feature that's supposed to be transparent until needed.
entries.compares: Every output sample of a signal already at half the ceiling level, against the corresponding input sample, requiring bit-identical output (max diff <1e-7) — the untouched input is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The safety limiter is completely transparent when the signal never threatens the ceiling — normal-level playing is never colored or altered by the presence of the safety limiter, only genuinely hot signals are.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / stereo-linked: same gain applied to both channels (ratio preserved)
entries.what: Feeds L=4.0/R=1.0 (a 4:1 same-sign imbalance) and asserts the settled L/R output ratio still equals 4.0 within 1e-3 — i.e. the SAME gain reduction is applied to both channels rather than each channel limited independently.
entries.why: Stereo-linked (not independent per-channel) limiting is required to preserve the stereo image — independent limiting would narrow/widen the image asymmetrically whenever one channel is louder than the other.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter is applying different gain reduction to L and R — a loud transient on one channel would narrow or skew the stereo image instead of the whole stereo signal being uniformly pulled down.
entries.compares: The settled L/R sample ratio after limiting a loud-L/quiet-R signal (4.0/1.0) against the literal input ratio 4.0 (within 1e-3) — the limiter's gain must be identical on both channels (stereo-linked), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: When the limiter engages on a loud stereo signal, it applies the same gain reduction to both channels rather than gain-riding them independently — the stereo image (left/right balance) is preserved even while the limiter is actively working, not smeared or pulled off-center.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / gain reduction reports 0 below ceiling, >0 above
entries.what: Asserts gainReductionDb() reads under 0.01 dB for a quiet (0.1xceiling) signal and over 1.0 dB for a loud (5.0 amplitude) signal.
entries.why: gainReductionDb() is the metering value a UI would show (e.g. a limiter-activity LED/meter) — it must accurately distinguish 'not working' from 'actively working', not just silently do the right thing to the audio while misreporting its own state.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter's self-reported gain-reduction metering is wrong — any UI meter showing limiter activity to the user would be misleading, even if the actual audio processing (tested separately) is correct.
entries.compares: gainReductionDb() against a fixed floor of <0.01 for a quiet (0.1x ceiling) signal and against a fixed floor of >1.0 for a loud (5.0 amplitude) signal — a metering-contract check against fixed thresholds, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The limiter's gain-reduction meter (what a user would see on a GR display) genuinely reads near-zero when the limiter is idle and a meaningful positive value when it's actively working — the metering is trustworthy feedback about whether the limiter is doing anything.

entries.key: SafetyLimiter / release recovers after a loud burst
entries.what: After a loud burst pushes gain reduction up, feeds ~320 ms (60 blocks) of quiet signal (>=4 release time-constants at an 80 ms release) and asserts gain reduction has fallen to under 10% of its post-burst value.
entries.why: A limiter that never releases would leave audio permanently quieter after any loud transient — release-time correctness is what makes the limiter feel transparent again once the danger has passed, rather than leaving a lingering ducking artifact.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter's gain reduction isn't recovering after a loud burst passes — audio would stay audibly quieter long after the transient that triggered limiting is gone, a noticeable 'stuck ducking' artifact.
entries.compares: gainReductionDb() after ~320ms of quiet following a loud burst (>=4 release time-constants at an 80ms release) against the literal expectation of falling below 10% of its post-burst value — a recovery-time contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: After the limiter engages on a loud passage, it releases and returns to transparent operation within a reasonable time once the signal quiets down — the limiter doesn't get stuck clamped down long after the loud moment has passed.

entries.key: Halfband2x / upsample passband (200 Hz @ 48k) preserved, ~unity
entries.what: Upsamples a 200 Hz tone at 48k through Halfband2x and, after warm-up, measures its magnitude at the corresponding 2x-domain frequency (f/2) via a Goertzel-style DFT; asserts the gain is between 0.9 and 1.1 (near unity).
entries.why: Halfband2x is the resampler used by every voice's OS path (Q12/Q23); in-band content must pass through the upsample stage essentially unchanged in level, or every oversampled tier would subtly alter the base signal's loudness.
entries.deviationMeans: The upsample stage is adding gain or attenuation to in-band content — any oversampled voice (os2/4/8) would sound louder or quieter than the os1 base case for reasons unrelated to the filter itself.
entries.compares: DFT magnitude of a 200Hz tone (measured at its shifted 2x-rate bin) after upsampling, against a fixed analytic band of 0.9-1.1 (unity gain) — a passband-transparency contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The 2x upsampling stage used throughout the oversampling chain doesn't color or attenuate in-band audio — content well within the audible/base-rate passband survives upsampling at unity gain.

entries.key: Halfband2x / downsample rejects an image above base-Nyquist
entries.what: Feeds a 0.30 cyc/sample tone (2x-domain, above base-Nyquist) into downsample and asserts the peak magnitude anywhere in the base-rate output (which would show the image aliased to 0.40 normalized) is below -80 dB.
entries.why: This is the anti-aliasing guarantee that makes oversampling worthwhile at all — Q12's OS policy depends on the downsample stage actually rejecting images, not just decimating blindly.
entries.deviationMeans: The halfband downsample filter's stopband rejection has degraded below 80 dB — oversampled voices would leak audible aliased images back into the base-rate output, undermining the entire point of the OS tiers.
entries.compares: Peak DFT magnitude (swept 0-0.5 cycles/sample) of a tone that would alias into the base-rate band, converted to dB, against a fixed analytic ceiling of <-80 dB image rejection — the threshold IS the anti-aliasing contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Downsampling from 2x back to base rate genuinely removes ultrasonic/aliasing-prone content above base-Nyquist before decimating — the oversampling chain's anti-aliasing filter does its job, protecting against audible aliasing artifacts.

entries.key: Halfband2x / golden equivalence vector (guards optimizations against numeric drift)
entries.what: Runs deterministic LCG noise through upsample then downsample and compares specific output-sample indices against reference values captured 2026-07-02 from the pre-optimization implementation, within 2e-5 float tolerance.
entries.why: This is the exact guard Q23 describes: the Q23 zero-skipping halfband optimization (exploiting exact halfband zero taps) is only legal because it is bit-transparent with the original dense-convolution implementation — this test is what keeps 'faster' from silently becoming 'different'.
entries.deviationMeans: The optimized halfband resampler no longer matches the reference numerically — an oversampled tier is now producing a different sound, not just a cheaper computation; per the code's own comment, a DELIBERATE redesign must recapture these vectors and say so in the diff.
entries.compares: 6 specific up-sampled and 6 specific down-sampled output samples (indices captured 2026-07-02 from the reference pre-optimization implementation, driven by a deterministic LCG noise sequence seeded 12345) against literal float constants baked directly into the test source, within 2e-5 — a golden vector embedded in code (not a separate golden file), explicitly designed to catch numeric drift from future optimizations.
entries.succeedingMeans: An optimized rewrite of the halfband filter (e.g. exploiting the zero taps of a true halfband) must reproduce the exact same numeric output as the reference implementation — any accidental behavior change from a performance optimization is caught immediately, not discovered later as an audible regression.

entries.key: Halfband2x / round-trip reconstructs the input at an integer base-sample delay
entries.what: Round-trips a 200 Hz tone through upsample then downsample, finds the best-aligning integer delay D by cross-correlation (scanning 0-80 samples), asserts the alignment gain is within 0.05 of unity, and that the residual reconstruction error after aligning is below -40 dB NRMSE.
entries.why: Guarantees the combined up/down halfband pair introduces only a clean INTEGER-sample delay (the even-phase decimation design), not a fractional-sample or time-varying offset, which would break any code doing sample-accurate alignment across the OS boundary.
entries.deviationMeans: The round-trip through the resampler pair is no longer a clean integer-delay passband reconstruction — either the group delay became non-integer/fractional, or passband fidelity through the pair has degraded.
entries.compares: A cross-correlation-derived best-fit integer delay D (searched 0-80 samples) between round-tripped output and delayed input, requiring the aligned gain be ~1 (within 0.05) and the normalized RMS reconstruction error be <-40 dB — analytic self-consistency (the input is its own reference after alignment), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Sending audio through upsample-then-downsample reconstructs it faithfully at a clean, whole-sample delay (no fractional-sample smearing) — the oversampling round-trip doesn't introduce timing artifacts or reconstruction noise into the signal.

entries.key: VoiceOversampler / factor 1 is identity (up then down)
entries.what: At oversampling factor 1 (no-op), processes a sine through processMonoUp then processStereoDown and asserts the round-tripped output matches the original input within 1e-5.
entries.why: Factor 1 must be a true identity path — any coloration at the 'off' oversampling setting would mean the default (most commonly used) OS tier is subtly altering every voice's sound.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'no oversampling' (factor 1) path is not a clean identity — the most commonly used OS tier (the default) would be subtly coloring every voice's output.
entries.compares: Every downsampled output sample at oversampling factor 1 against the original input sample, requiring bit-level equality within 1e-5 — the input itself is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Setting the oversampling factor to 1 (effectively off) is a true identity pass-through — a user who leaves oversampling at its lowest setting gets unmodified audio, not a filtered-and-reconstructed copy.

entries.key: VoiceOversampler / round-trip latency matches the table (impulse, 2x/4x/8x)
entries.what: For factors {2,4,8}, round-trips a unit impulse through up/down sampling and asserts the output's peak position exactly matches VoiceOversampler::latencyBaseSamples(factor).
entries.why: The reported latency table (used by PluginLifecycleTests' PDC-reporting test to inform the host) must match the ACTUAL measured round-trip delay of the resampler pair, or host latency compensation would be wrong.
entries.deviationMeans: The oversampler's actual round-trip delay no longer matches its own reported latency table — a host relying on the reported PDC latency (per PluginLifecycleTests) would misalign this track against others, since the real delay differs from what's being reported.
entries.compares: The sample index of the peak response to an impulse, for each of factors 2/4/8, against VoiceOversampler::latencyBaseSamples(f) — the oversampler's own published latency-lookup table is the reference, no separate golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The latency the plugin reports to the host for each oversampling factor is the actual, measured latency of the oversampling filters — plugin delay compensation (PDC) in the DAW will be accurate, not just a guessed/approximate number.

entries.key: CmajorSvfAdapter / adapter LP passes lows, attenuates highs
entries.what: Measures RMS response of SvfLinearAdapter (LP tap, cutoff 1000 Hz) at 200 Hz vs 8000 Hz; asserts both are finite, the low passes with RMS > 0.1, and the high is attenuated to less than half the low's RMS.
entries.why: Basic sanity gate on the lower-level Cmajor SVF linear-core adapter itself, one layer below the full CmajorFilterModel/FilterModel-interface test — isolates whether a failure is in the raw adapter math or the interface wiring around it.
entries.deviationMeans: The Cmajor SVF linear-core adapter's basic LP shape is broken at the lowest level (before any FilterModel interface concerns) — check this before assuming the bug is in higher-level wiring.
entries.compares: RMS at 200Hz (passband) against a fixed floor (>0.1) and RMS at 8kHz (stopband) against the passband RMS scaled by a fixed factor (high < low*0.5), for the Cmajor-generated SVF adapter — an analytic LP contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-codegen'd SVF filter adapter (the coexistence spike's DSP-in-Cmajor path) behaves like a genuine low-pass filter on its own — the generated code isn't broken at the most basic filter-shape level before even comparing it to the C++ reference.

entries.key: CmajorSvfEquivalence / Cmajor SVF matches NlSvfCell linear core within 0.5 dB
entries.what: Cross-checks SvfLinearAdapter (Cmajor) against NlSvfCell's linear core (resSat=0, C++) across 3 cutoffs x 3 taps (LP/HP/BP) x 5 probe frequencies (45 combinations), asserting every pairwise magnitude delta is under 0.5 dB.
entries.why: The equivalence proof required by ADR-0012's hybrid model — the Cmajor port of the SVF linear core must sound identical to the proven C++ NlSvfCell it's meant to replace/coexist with, across modes and cutoffs, not just at one operating point.
entries.deviationMeans: The Cmajor SVF linear core has diverged from the reference C++ NlSvfCell at some cutoff/tap/frequency combination — the hybrid engine's Cmajor path would produce an audibly different filter response from the equivalent C++ path.
entries.compares: Magnitude response (dB) of the Cmajor-generated SvfLinearAdapter against the hand-written C++ NlSvfCell (linear core, res-sat off), swept across 3 cutoffs x 3 taps (LP/HP/BP) x 5 probe frequencies (45 combinations), requiring agreement within 0.5 dB at every point — cross-method/reference-implementation agreement, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated filter code produces audibly the same frequency response as the trusted hand-written C++ filter across its whole operating range — a Cmajor-authored DSP block can be trusted to sound identical to its C++ equivalent, the core claim the Cmajor coexistence spike needs to validate.

entries.key: CmajorFilterModel / CmajorSvfFilter runs through the FilterModel interface (stereo, param-driven)
entries.what: Runs CmajorSvfFilter through the FilterModel interface (makeState/reset/setCommon/processStereo) at 200 Hz and 8 kHz probe tones; asserts L==R for identical input (within 1e-6), and that the 8 kHz RMS is under half the 200 Hz RMS (LP attenuation through the full interface path).
entries.why: Proves the Cmajor SVF is not just numerically correct in isolation but correctly wired into the FilterModel abstraction (ADR-0011's append-only registry) that VoiceManager/Layer actually calls — the interface boundary, not just the DSP math.
entries.deviationMeans: The Cmajor SVF's FilterModel-interface wiring is broken — even if the underlying Cmajor DSP is correct, a live voice routed through this adapter wouldn't filter correctly.
entries.compares: RMS at 200Hz (passband, >floor) vs 8kHz (stopband, < half the passband RMS) through the full FilterModel-interface wrapper around the Cmajor adapter, plus an exact L==R equality check (within 1e-6) for identical stereo input — an analytic LP contract plus a channel-symmetry check, no golden file.
