entries.succeedingMeans: A Cmajor-generated filter can be wrapped in the same FilterModel interface every other spine filter uses (Huggett, Moog) and behaves correctly through it — Cmajor DSP is a genuine drop-in candidate for the spine architecture, not a special case requiring its own integration path.

entries.key: CmajorFilterModel / processStereo does not allocate on the audio thread
entries.what: Runs an 8192-sample block through processStereo without any makeState/new call in the hot path and asserts the output stays finite; documented as a structural assertion backed primarily by code review, not a true allocation-tracking test.
entries.why: L7's heap-free, in-place model-slot invariant (the hot-swap crossfade design) requires every FilterModel's processStereo to never allocate; a Cmajor-generated adapter is exactly the kind of code where a codegen change could quietly reintroduce an allocation.
entries.deviationMeans: This test alone would not catch a reintroduced allocation (it's a smoke/finite check, not an allocation profiler) — but a failure here signals the Cmajor SVF adapter has broken more fundamentally; pair with manual profiling if allocation-safety is in question.
entries.compares: Nothing numeric — an 8192-sample block processed through an already-constructed state, checked only for a finite final sample; the real claim (no audio-thread allocation) is documented as verified by code review, not by this runtime assertion.
entries.succeedingMeans: Running a large block through the Cmajor filter's hot path doesn't crash or produce non-finite output; the actual real-time-safety guarantee (no heap allocation in processStereo) is a code-review claim this test can only weakly corroborate, not independently prove — a stronger allocation-detection test would be needed to catch a real regression here.

entries.key: CmajorSvfPerf / 256-voice perf: Cmajor adapter vs NlSvfCell (ratio is a decision input)
entries.what: Times 256 voices x 200 blocks of 128 samples through the Cmajor SvfLinearAdapter vs the C++ NlSvfCell and prints their wall-clock ratio and sizeof() to stdout; asserts only that both paths produce finite output and non-zero elapsed time — there is no pass/fail perf threshold.
entries.why: A decision-input measurement for ADR-0012 (Cmajor coexistence): the printed cmaj/cpp ratio informs whether Cmajor-generated DSP is cheap enough to use at the 64-voice product target (Q2), not a correctness gate.
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here means the harness itself broke (non-finite output or zero elapsed time), not a performance regression — perf regressions must be read from the printed ratio in the test log, not from pass/fail.
entries.compares: Nothing pass/fail beyond finiteness and non-zero elapsed time for both paths; the actual output is a printed wall-clock ratio (Cmajor-adapter time / NlSvfCell time) for 256 simulated voices — a measurement harness feeding an architectural decision, not a regression gate with a threshold.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is gated here; this produces the wall-clock performance ratio between Cmajor-generated and hand-written C++ filter code at realistic voice counts, which is raw input data for the ADR-0012 Cmajor-vs-JUCE decision — a change in this ratio should prompt a human to re-examine that decision, not fail CI.

entries.key: NlSvfEquivalence / Cmajor NlSvf matches NlSvfCell (resonance saturator on), per-sample
entries.what: Cross-checks NlSvfAdapter (Cmajor, resonance saturator engaged) against NlSvfCell (C++) across 3 cutoffs x 3 resonance/resSat values x 3 amplitudes x 3 taps (81 combinations), asserting per-sample max error stays under 2e-3 in every case; notes a fallback to harmonic-amplitude comparison if the tanh/FMA bound proves flaky.
entries.why: This is the nonlinear (resonance-saturator-engaged) equivalence proof required for ADR-0012 — extends CmajorSvfEquivalenceTests' linear-only proof to the actual nonlinear resonance path that gives Huggett/Moog their character.
entries.deviationMeans: The Cmajor port of the nonlinear resonance-saturator SVF has diverged from the C++ reference at some cutoff/resonance/amplitude/tap combination — the hybrid engine's Cmajor path would sound different specifically when resonance is engaged, the exact regime where filter character matters most.
entries.compares: Per-sample output of the Cmajor-generated nonlinear SVF adapter against the C++ NlSvfCell (resonance saturator engaged, matching resonance==resSat), swept across 3 cutoffs x 3 resonance/saturation levels x 3 amplitudes x 3 taps (81 combinations), requiring worst-case per-sample error <2e-3 — cross-method/reference-implementation agreement with an explicit documented fallback (switch to a harmonic-amplitude comparison) if transcendental-function drift makes this bound flaky.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated nonlinear (saturating) filter matches the trusted C++ implementation sample-for-sample across resonance, drive, and level combinations — the nonlinear DSP path (not just the linear core) is validated as a faithful Cmajor port, extending the coexistence spike's evidence to the harder, self-oscillation-adjacent case.

entries.key: AsymDriveEquivalence / Cmajor AsymDrive matches AsymSaturator
entries.what: Runs a 220 Hz tone at 4 drive settings (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0) through both the Cmajor AsymDriveAdapter and the C++ AsymSaturator, asserting per-sample max error stays below 1e-3 at every drive level (8192 samples each).
entries.why: ADR-0012's Cmajor/JUCE hybrid is only safe if the Cmajor-ported nonlinear stages are numerically equivalent to their proven C++ originals — AsymSaturator is one of the three gray-box tanh stages from Q15's nonlinear-filter-modeling; this is the bit-level trust bridge.
entries.deviationMeans: The Cmajor port of the asymmetric drive stage has diverged from the reference C++ implementation — the hybrid engine's Cmajor path would sound subtly different from the proven/measured C++ path, undermining ADR-0012's equivalence guarantee.
entries.compares: Per-sample output of the Cmajor-generated AsymDriveAdapter against the C++ AsymSaturator at 4 drive levels, requiring worst-case per-sample error <1e-3 — cross-method/reference-implementation agreement with a documented harmonic-comparison fallback if pure per-sample error proves too strict for tanh/pow last-bit differences.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated asymmetric drive/waveshaping stage matches the trusted C++ implementation sample-for-sample — the nonlinear waveshaper, not just the filters, is validated as a faithful Cmajor port for the coexistence spike.

entries.key: WtOsc / table pushed from C++ plays back as the expected single-cycle waveform
entries.what: Pushes a single-cycle sine table (via setTable) into WtOscAdapter (a Cmajor wavetable oscillator), plays back at 440 Hz, and asserts via Goertzel bin analysis that energy at 440 Hz exceeds energy at an unrelated 1500 Hz bin by more than 8x, with non-trivial RMS (>0.1).
entries.why: Proves the C++-to-Cmajor wavetable HANDOFF actually works (the table crosses the language boundary and is used for playback), not just that the Cmajor oscillator can play a hardcoded internal waveform — this is the specific mechanism any future VAST wavetable-source block (Q4's synth-only-now scope, deferred sample/keymap sources) would depend on.
entries.deviationMeans: A C++-pushed wavetable is not being used correctly by the Cmajor oscillator adapter — the cross-language table-handoff mechanism is broken, which would block any wavetable-based VAST source block from working correctly.
entries.compares: Goertzel single-bin energy at the played frequency (440Hz) against energy at an unrelated frequency (1500Hz), requiring the played-frequency energy exceed the off-frequency energy by a factor of >8, plus overall RMS against a fixed non-silence floor (>0.1) — an analytic spectral-concentration contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A wavetable authored/computed in C++ and pushed into the Cmajor-generated wavetable oscillator plays back as the intended pitched waveform — the C++-to-Cmajor data handoff for wavetables genuinely works, not just Cmajor-internal state.

entries.key: NlSvfPerf / 256-voice nonlinear perf: copy vs lean(zero-copy) adapter vs NlSvfCell
entries.what: Times three implementations at 512 channel-streams of work (256 voices, fairness-matched: NlSvfCell processes stereo per call so Cmajor mono adapters run at 2x count): the copy-based NlSvfAdapter, the zero-copy NlSvfLeanAdapter, and the C++ NlSvfCell baseline; prints ratios and sizeof() for each; asserts only that all three produce finite output and non-zero elapsed time.
entries.why: A decision-input measurement distinguishing 'Cmajor codegen is inherently slower' from 'the copy-based adapter's copying overhead is the cost' — the lean/zero-copy variant isolates whether avoiding per-block copies closes the gap to the C++ baseline, informing which adapter style to standardize on for the 64-voice target.
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here means the harness itself broke (non-finite output), not a performance regression — perf conclusions must be read from the printed copy-vs-lean-vs-cpp ratios in the test log, per the same convention as the other Cmajor perf tests.
entries.compares: Nothing pass/fail beyond finiteness and non-zero elapsed time for all three paths; the actual output is a printed wall-clock comparison across copy-adapter, zero-copy(lean)-adapter, and C++ NlSvfCell at an apples-to-apples 512 channel-streams of equal work — a measurement harness, not a regression gate.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is gated here; this produces the performance delta between a naive copy-based Cmajor adapter and a zero-copy 'lean' adapter design against the C++ baseline, informing whether the copy overhead of the straightforward Cmajor integration is worth optimizing away before committing to the architecture.

entries.key: NlSvfFusedPerf / fused NlSvfDrive matches C++ NlSvfCell->AsymSaturator chain (per-sample)
entries.what: Compares the fused NlSvfDriveLeanAdapter (SVF+resonance-saturator+drive in one Cmajor advance) against the equivalent C++ chain (NlSvfCell then AsymSaturator) on a 220 Hz tone; asserts per-sample max error is under 2e-3.
entries.why: Proves the FUSED multi-stage Cmajor processor (the realistic v6 shape per the code comment: paying per-advance overhead once for a whole chain, not once per node) is numerically equivalent to composing the same stages separately in C++ — a correctness prerequisite before trusting its perf numbers.
entries.deviationMeans: The fused Cmajor SVF+saturator+drive processor no longer matches composing the same three stages in C++ — a v6-shaped fused processor would sound different from its unfused equivalent, undermining the fusion optimization's validity.
entries.compares: Per-sample output of a single fused Cmajor processor (SVF + resonance saturator + drive in one advance) against chaining the separate C++ NlSvfCell and AsymSaturator classes, requiring worst-case per-sample error <2e-3 — cross-method/reference-implementation agreement, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Fusing multiple DSP stages into a single Cmajor-generated processor (the realistic v6 multi-stage-chain shape) produces the same audio as running the equivalent separate C++ stages in sequence — fusing stages for performance doesn't change the sound.

entries.key: NlSvfFusedPerf / fused-processor perf vs block size (per-advance overhead amortization)
entries.what: Times the fused adapter (512 mono channel-streams) vs the equivalent C++ chain across block sizes {64,128,256,512} for a fixed total sample count, printing the fused/cpp ratio at each block size; asserts only that the accumulated sink value is finite — no pass/fail perf threshold.
entries.why: A decision-input measurement (like CmajorSvfPerfTests): if the fused/cpp ratio falls as block size grows, the gap is per-advance overhead (amortizable by using bigger blocks), not fundamental per-sample codegen cost — informs whether the v6 fused-processor strategy is viable at the 64-voice target (Q2).
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here means the harness itself broke (non-finite sink), not a perf regression — read the printed block-size-vs-ratio table in the test log to assess actual performance trends, per the same convention as CmajorSvfPerfTests.
entries.compares: Nothing pass/fail beyond overall finiteness; the actual output is a printed table of fused-vs-chained wall-clock time and their ratio across 4 block sizes (64/128/256/512) for equal total work — a measurement harness isolating per-advance overhead, not a regression gate.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is gated here; this produces evidence for whether the Cmajor per-advance call overhead (measured separately per-stage as ~1.95x at block 128) amortizes away when multiple stages are fused into one processor and driven with larger blocks — informs whether fusing is the right performance strategy for the v6 Cmajor architecture.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / bin-aligned tone is a single FFT bin
entries.what: Generates a bin-aligned sine at bin 75 (N=16384) and asserts the fundamental bin's magnitude exceeds 1.0 while every other bin stays below 1e-4x the fundamental (no spectral leakage).
entries.why: Foundational trust test for the SignalGen/Spectrum test-DSP primitives themselves — every other harness test that measures 'energy at a specific bin' depends on bin-aligned tones genuinely landing in exactly one bin with no leakage.
entries.deviationMeans: The test-DSP signal generation or FFT primitives have a leakage bug — any test elsewhere that assumes a bin-aligned tone stays in one bin (which is most of the harmonic/aliasing measurement suite) would get corrupted readings.
entries.compares: FFT magnitude at the target bin (75) against a fixed floor (>1.0), and the loudest OTHER bin against the target bin scaled down by a fixed factor (<1e-4x, i.e. -80dB leakage floor) — a self-test of the testdsp::SignalGen/Spectrum helper library itself, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared test-infrastructure signal generator and FFT wrapper (used by dozens of other DSP tests to measure spectra) genuinely produces a clean, leak-free single-bin tone — every test elsewhere that trusts this helper for spectral measurement is standing on verified ground, not an unverified assumption.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / rms of unit sine is ~0.707
entries.what: Asserts Spectrum::rms of a unit-amplitude sine reads 0.70710677 (1/sqrt(2)) within 1e-3.
entries.why: Exact-answer proof of the RMS primitive used throughout the harness's own test-DSP layer (distinct from testdsp::Level's RMS, tested in SyntheticReferenceTests/LevelExtractors) — a basic building block many spectral tests are built on.
entries.deviationMeans: The Spectrum::rms primitive itself is wrong — a foundational test-DSP-layer bug that would silently corrupt any test using this specific RMS helper.
entries.compares: testdsp::Spectrum::rms() of a unit-amplitude sine against the literal analytic constant 0.70710677 (1/sqrt(2), within 1e-3) — the textbook RMS-of-a-sine identity, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared RMS-measurement helper used throughout the test suite computes RMS correctly against the known analytic answer — every other test's RMS-based level claims (audibility floors, gain measurements) are built on a verified primitive.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / allFinite catches NaN
entries.what: Asserts Spectrum::allFinite returns false for a buffer containing a NaN.
entries.why: allFinite is the universal 'did this render blow up' check used across dozens of tests in this suite (e.g. HuggettBoundedResonanceTests, MoogLadderTests) — it must genuinely detect NaN, not silently pass corrupted buffers.
entries.deviationMeans: The finite-check primitive itself is failing to detect NaN — every test in the suite relying on allFinite/isfinite-style checks to catch a DSP blow-up would be blind to it.
entries.compares: testdsp::Spectrum::allFinite() on a buffer containing a deliberately injected NaN, against the literal expectation false — a self-test of the finiteness-checking helper, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared NaN/Inf-detection helper (used by every stability test in the suite) genuinely catches a broken buffer — every 'output stays finite' claim elsewhere in the suite is backed by a helper proven to actually detect the failure it's meant to catch.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / M3 inharmonicDb ~ floor for a pure tone
entries.what: Asserts Metrics::inharmonicDb of a clean bin-aligned tone reads below -100 dB (i.e. no measurable inharmonic energy in a genuinely pure tone).
entries.why: M3 is the inharmonic-energy metric used by the OverdriveDiagnosticTests artifact score and AliasSplitTests — this proves it reports 'clean' on a signal that genuinely is clean, the negative-control baseline for those diagnostic tools.
entries.deviationMeans: The inharmonic-energy metric is reporting spurious inharmonic content on a signal that has none — every diagnostic tool built on this metric (aliasing/artifact scoring) would show false positives.
entries.compares: testdsp::Metrics::inharmonicDb() of a pure bin-aligned tone against a fixed noise-floor ceiling of <-100 dB — a self-test of the inharmonic-energy metric on a known-clean input, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared inharmonic-distortion metric (M3, used to detect aliasing/artifacts elsewhere in the suite) correctly reads near-zero for genuinely clean audio — a real artifact detected by this metric elsewhere in the suite is a real artifact, not a false positive from the metric itself.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / M6 thdPlusNDb ~ floor for a pure tone
entries.what: Asserts Metrics::thdPlusNDb of a clean bin-aligned tone reads below -100 dB.
entries.why: M6 is the THD+N metric used by RenderFingerprintTests' golden checks and elsewhere — proves it correctly reports near-zero distortion on a genuinely clean signal.
entries.deviationMeans: The THD+N metric is manufacturing spurious distortion readings on a clean tone — every golden check or gate using thdPlusNDb (including the anti-drift RenderFingerprint goldens) would report false drift.
entries.compares: testdsp::Metrics::thdPlusNDb() of a pure bin-aligned tone against a fixed noise-floor ceiling of <-100 dB — a self-test of the THD+N metric on a known-clean input, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared THD+N distortion metric (M6, used throughout the suite to quantify harmonic distortion) correctly reads near-zero for genuinely clean audio — distortion numbers reported elsewhere in the suite reflect the device under test, not measurement-tool noise.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / M8 maxDiff is zero for identical buffers, exact for a known offset
entries.what: Asserts Metrics::maxDiff returns exactly 0.0 for two identical buffers and exactly 0.25 after uniformly offsetting one buffer by 0.25.
entries.why: M8 (maxDiff) is the equivalence-proof primitive used throughout the Cmajor-vs-C++ equivalence tests (AsymDriveEquivalenceTests, NlSvfEquivalenceTests, CmajorSvfEquivalenceTests) — this is its own correctness proof before being trusted for ADR-0012's bit-transparency guarantees.
entries.deviationMeans: The maxDiff primitive is miscalculating — every Cmajor/C++ equivalence proof in the suite (the entire trust chain behind ADR-0012's hybrid architecture) would be reporting wrong numbers.
entries.compares: testdsp::Metrics::maxDiff() on two identical buffers against the literal value 0.0 (exact), and on a buffer offset by a known constant (+0.25) against the literal value 0.25 (within 1e-6) — an exact analytic self-test of the diff metric, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared max-absolute-difference metric (M8, used by every cross-method and reference-equivalence test in the suite) computes exactly what it claims to — every 'matches within X' claim elsewhere in the suite is measured by a metric proven arithmetically correct.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / stereoCorrelation = 1 identical, ~ -1 inverted
entries.what: Asserts Metrics::stereoCorrelation reads 1.0 for identical L/R channels and -1.0 after inverting R.
entries.why: stereoCorrelation is used by tests like SpineHuggettHarnessTests' 'stereo L==R correlation' check — must correctly distinguish identical from inverted stereo before being trusted for those correlation-based assertions.
entries.deviationMeans: The stereo-correlation primitive is wrong at its most basic (identical vs inverted) cases — any test checking stereo-channel correlation (e.g. proving L/R process identically for a mono-summed input) would be unreliable.
entries.compares: testdsp::Metrics::stereoCorrelation() on identical L/R channels against the literal value 1.0 (within 1e-4), and on inverted-R channels against the literal value -1.0 (within 1e-4) — the textbook correlation-coefficient identities, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared stereo-correlation metric (used to verify L/R independence or coherence elsewhere in the suite) reads the correct textbook extremes — stereo-behavior claims elsewhere in the suite rest on a correctly-computed correlation measure.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / decimator preserves an in-band tone (reconstruction < -100 dB error)
entries.what: Decimates a 16x-oversampled 60-bin tone down to base rate via testdsp::Reference::decimate (257-tap FIR) and compares its steady-state RMS (after skipping the ~16-sample group-delay transient) against a directly-generated base-rate reference tone; asserts RMS agreement within 5e-3.
entries.why: testdsp::Reference::decimate is the ground-truth downsampling tool used by M4's NSR (noise-to-signal) comparisons and other tests needing a trusted high-rate-to-base-rate reference — must itself be proven transparent on in-band content before being trusted as 'truth'.
entries.deviationMeans: The reference decimator used as ground truth elsewhere in the suite (e.g. the M4 hard-clip-vs-tanh aliasing comparison) is itself introducing error — any test comparing against 'truth' via this decimator would be comparing against a flawed reference.
entries.compares: RMS of a decimated high-rate tone (after skipping the 257-tap FIR's ~16-sample group delay) against RMS of the same tone rendered natively at the base rate, within a fixed tolerance of 5e-3 — a self-test of the testdsp::Reference::decimate helper against an independently-generated reference tone, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared high-rate-to-base-rate decimation helper (used as the 'ground truth' oracle for alias/distortion measurements elsewhere in the suite) is itself faithful and doesn't introduce its own artifacts — measurements elsewhere that compare against a decimated reference are comparing against a trustworthy oracle.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / M4 NSR: hard clip aliases worse than soft tanh (DAFx-16 ordering)
entries.what: Compares noise-to-signal ratio (aliasing) of hard clipping vs soft tanh saturation on the same 3 kHz tone (16x-oversampled ground truth vs base-rate device-under-test, decimated for comparison); asserts hard clip's NSR exceeds soft tanh's by more than 6 dB (measured ~11 dB margin).
entries.why: Validates the M4 metric reproduces a well-known DSP-literature result (the DAFx-16 ordering: harder nonlinearities alias more than softer ones) — this is the test-DSP layer's own credibility check before M4 is trusted to characterize real filter drive stages.
entries.deviationMeans: The M4 aliasing-comparison metric no longer reproduces the textbook hard-vs-soft-clipping aliasing ordering — a fundamental credibility failure for the tool used to compare drive-stage aliasing behavior (e.g. Huggett's tanh-class saturators, Q15).
entries.compares: Noise-to-signal ratio (NSR, measuring aliasing) of a hard-clipped tone against a soft-tanh-clipped tone (both processed at base rate then compared to an oversampled decimated reference), requiring hard-clip NSR exceed soft-clip NSR by >6 dB — an ordering/relative contract matching the published DAFx-16 result that hard clipping aliases more than soft saturation, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared aliasing-measurement machinery (M4/NSR) correctly ranks a known-worse nonlinearity (hard clipping) as measurably worse than a known-better one (soft tanh) — matching a documented published result gives confidence the metric measures real aliasing, not an artifact of the measurement method itself.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / Gate passes within bound
entries.what: Calls testdsp::Gate::check with a value of -70.0 against a -60.0 Max-direction threshold and confirms it passes (a demo/self-test of the Gate helper itself).
entries.why: Gate::check is the assertion-wrapper primitive used by every gating test in the characterization harness (CharacterizationGateTests, RunnerLevelTests, etc.) — this proves its basic pass logic works correctly.
entries.deviationMeans: The Gate helper's basic Max-direction pass/fail logic is broken — every gate in the characterization suite built on Gate::check would have unreliable pass/fail semantics.
entries.compares: testdsp::Gate::check() with a measured value of -70 against a threshold of -60 (Max direction), which should pass since -70 <= -60 — a self-test of the shared pass/fail gate-checking helper itself, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared threshold-gating helper (used throughout the characterization/battery tests to turn a measurement into pass/fail) correctly evaluates a passing case — every battery/characterization gate elsewhere in the suite is built on a helper proven to gate correctly.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / Response::magDb LP passband matches Cytomic analytic (CellAdapter, fc=1000 Hz)
entries.what: Measures a CellAdapter-wrapped linear SVF (res=0, k=2.0 damping) at 5 passband frequencies (100-1000 Hz) via testdsp::Response::magDb and gates each against the exact analytic 2-pole LP formula within 0.5 dB, printing all measured/analytic/error values.
entries.why: Validates the Response::magDb measurement primitive (distinct from SteppedSine's transfer method) against a known analytic answer — a second, independent proof of the harness's core magnitude-measurement machinery.
entries.deviationMeans: The Response::magDb primitive disagrees with the exact analytic LP formula in the passband — since this is a different code path from SteppedSine::transfer, a failure here (while SteppedSine passes) would isolate the bug to this specific measurement helper.
entries.compares: testdsp::Response::magDb() measured response of a linear-regime SVF (CellAdapter, res=0, small amplitude) against the closed-form 2-pole analog LP formula |H(u)|_dB = -10*log10((1-u^2)^2 + (k*u)^2) with k=2.0 (from Q=0.5 at res=0), at 5 frequencies up to fc, gated at 0.5 dB via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared Response::magDb measurement helper (used throughout the suite to measure any filter's frequency response) agrees with textbook analog filter theory in the passband of a known-simple filter configuration — response-shape claims made elsewhere in the suite using this helper are measuring against a tool verified against first-principles math.

entries.key: TestDspSelf / GoldenIO round-trips a key,value map
entries.what: Saves a 3-entry key/value map (including a zero value) to a temp CSV via testdsp::GoldenIO::save, reloads it, and asserts all 3 keys and their exact values (within 1e-9) round-trip.
entries.why: GoldenIO is the persistence mechanism behind EVERY golden-baseline check in the suite (CharacterizationGateTests, RenderFingerprintTests) — a round-trip bug here would corrupt every committed golden file's read/write path.
entries.deviationMeans: The golden-file save/load mechanism itself is lossy or broken — every golden-regression test in the suite (which spans filter characterization AND the anti-drift sound fingerprints) would be reading or writing corrupted baseline data.
entries.compares: A key,value map saved to and reloaded from a temp CSV file via testdsp::GoldenIO against the literal original values (1.5, -22.25, 0.0, each within 1e-9) — the original in-memory map is the reference, no external golden file (this IS the test of the golden-file mechanism itself).
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared golden-file save/load helper (used by every test elsewhere in the suite that persists or compares against a golden reference file) round-trips numeric data exactly — golden-file comparisons elsewhere in the suite aren't silently corrupted by the I/O mechanism itself.

entries.key: SteppedSine / identity passthrough: 0 dB, ~0 phase across the grid
entries.what: Measures a no-op Passthrough device at 100/1000/10000 Hz via testdsp::SteppedSine::transfer; asserts every magnitude is within 0.05 dB of 0 and every phase is within 0.01 rad of 0.
entries.why: Foundational trust test for the stepped-sine measurement method itself (one half of the dual-method gate) — proves it reads true unity/zero-phase on a device with a known, trivial answer before ever being applied to a real filter.
entries.deviationMeans: The stepped-sine measurement method itself is producing wrong magnitude or phase readings even for a trivial passthrough device — every stepped-sine-based measurement in the suite (which is most of the filter-validation harness) becomes suspect.
entries.compares: testdsp::SteppedSine::transfer() measured magnitude and phase for a no-op passthrough processor, against the literal expected values 0 dB (within 0.05) and 0 radians (within 0.01) at 3 frequencies — a self-test of the stepped-sine measurement helper on a known-trivial system, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared stepped-sine frequency-response measurement method (an alternative to the FFT/sweep methods, used for dual-method cross-checks elsewhere) correctly reads flat/zero-phase for a system that truly does nothing — response-shape claims made with this method elsewhere are measuring against a tool proven accurate on the trivial case.

entries.key: SteppedSine / matches Cytomic LP analytic at fc=1000 (CellAdapter, res=0)
entries.what: Measures a CellAdapter-wrapped linear SVF (Cytomic-style, fc=1000, res=0, k=2.0) at 100/300/700/1000 Hz and compares against the exact closed-form Cytomic LP magnitude formula; asserts agreement within 0.5 dB at every probe.
entries.why: Validates the stepped-sine method against a REAL (not trivial passthrough) analytic filter with a known closed-form answer — a stronger trust test than the identity-passthrough case since it exercises actual frequency-dependent filtering.
entries.deviationMeans: The stepped-sine method disagrees with the exact analytic Cytomic LP formula on a known linear filter — since this is a real (non-trivial) filter shape, this is a stronger signal than the passthrough test that the measurement method has a frequency-dependent bug.
entries.compares: testdsp::SteppedSine::transfer() measured magnitude of a linear-regime SVF (CellAdapter, res=0) against the same closed-form 2-pole analog LP formula used in TestDspSelfTests (k=2.0), at 4 frequencies, within 0.5 dB — an analytic closed-form comparison via a second (stepped-sine) measurement method, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The stepped-sine measurement method independently agrees with analog filter theory on a known-simple filter — this is the dual-method cross-check partner to the FFT-based Response::magDb test, giving two independently-implemented tools that both agree with first-principles math (and therefore with each other).

entries.key: Sweep / ESS deconvolves to a spectrally-flat band-limited impulse (identity system)
entries.what: Generates an ESS (exponential sine sweep) 20 Hz-24 kHz, deconvolves it with its own inverse filter (identity system: output==sweep), and asserts the resulting impulse response's magnitude spectrum is flat within 3 dB across [2*f0, f1/2] relative to its 1 kHz level.
entries.why: Proves the defining Farina-method property (the whole ESS/deconvolution measurement technique's validity) — a band-limited impulse's spectrum must be flat, not just that SOME impulse-like peak appears; the code comment explicitly notes a time-domain single-sample check would be physically wrong given the impulse's inherent tens-of-ms spread.
entries.deviationMeans: The ESS deconvolution's identity-system self-test no longer produces a flat spectrum — the entire ESS/Farina-method measurement technique (used by EssResponse and everything built on it, including the method-agreement gates) would be suspect at its mathematical foundation.
entries.compares: The windowed magnitude spectrum of an ESS-deconvolved impulse response (identity system: sweep deconvolved with its own inverse filter) at several octave-spaced frequencies, referenced to its value at 1kHz, requiring worst-case deviation <3 dB across the interior swept band (2*f0 to f1/2) — the Farina exponential-sine-sweep method's defining flatness property, an analytic/theoretical contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared ESS (exponential sine sweep) measurement method — the sweep-and-deconvolve alternative to FFT/stepped-sine for measuring frequency response — correctly recovers a flat, band-limited impulse for a system that is truly an identity (no coloration) — the third independent measurement method used elsewhere in the suite is itself verified sound.

entries.key: TransferFunction / unit impulse -> 0 dB flat, ~0 phase
entries.what: Computes TransferFunction::fromImpulse on a pure unit impulse at 100/1000/10000 Hz and asserts every magnitude reads within 1e-3 dB of 0 (flat, unity gain).
entries.why: An impulse is the identity system by definition (flat magnitude at all frequencies) — this is the foundational trust test for the FFT-based transfer-function extraction used by EssResponse and other impulse-response-based measurement tools.
entries.deviationMeans: The impulse-response-to-transfer-function extraction is wrong even for the trivial identity case — every measurement tool built on TransferFunction::fromImpulse (including EssResponse's deconvolution-based method) would be suspect.
entries.compares: testdsp::TransferFunction::fromImpulse() measured magnitude of a unit impulse (the identity system) against the literal expected value 0 dB (within 1e-3) at 3 frequencies — a self-test of the impulse-response-to-transfer-function helper on a known-trivial input, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared impulse-response analysis helper (used to derive frequency/phase/group-delay response elsewhere in the suite) correctly reads a flat, uncolored response for a genuine unit impulse — response measurements made from captured impulse responses elsewhere are trustworthy.

entries.key: TransferFunction / pure delay -> group delay equals the delay
entries.what: Builds an impulse response with a pure 24-sample delay and asserts the extracted group delay at 500/1000/2000/4000 Hz all equal 24/sr seconds within 1e-6.
entries.why: Group-delay extraction from a KNOWN, exact delay is the ground-truth proof this specific extraction path is correct — notably, this is the TransferFunction path (impulse-based), not the EssResponse/deconvolution path that Q20 flagged as currently unreliable for phase/group-delay.
entries.deviationMeans: Group-delay extraction from a clean impulse response is wrong — since this is a DIFFERENT code path from the EssResponse method Q20 already flags as unreliable, a failure here would indicate a second, independent group-delay bug, not the known Q20 limitation.
entries.compares: testdsp::TransferFunction::fromImpulse()'s computed group delay (seconds) for a pure N-sample-delayed impulse against the literal analytic expectation D/sr (24 samples at 96kHz, within 1e-6) — an exact analytic identity (a pure delay's group delay is constant and equals the delay), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared group-delay measurement helper correctly identifies a pure time delay as exactly what it is — phase/group-delay claims made elsewhere in the suite (e.g. latency verification) rest on a helper proven exact on the simplest possible case.

entries.key: MoogPipeline / adapter is in-place and embeds cheaply
entries.what: Asserts sizeof(MoogLadderAdapter) <= 1024 bytes, documenting that the small-block Cmajor codegen (maxFramesPerBlock=32, per the cmaj toolchain notes) keeps the generated adapter's footprint well under the default codegen's ~4-8 KB.
entries.why: The per-voice slot budget (Q18's kMaxSpineStateBytes=512) requires every FilterModel's footprint to be small; this pins the specific codegen choice (small maxFramesPerBlock) that makes the Cmajor-generated Moog adapter viable within that budget at all.
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog adapter's footprint has grown past the sanity threshold — likely the Cmajor codegen reverted to a larger maxFramesPerBlock, which would blow the per-voice state budget (Q18) once this adapter is used as a live FilterModel.
entries.compares: sizeof(MoogLadderAdapter) (the Cmajor-generated Moog adapter, using the small-block maxFramesPerBlock=32 codegen) against a fixed ceiling of <=1024 bytes — a footprint/de-risk contract for the small-block codegen approach, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated Moog filter's per-voice memory footprint stays small enough (under 1KB) to embed cheaply per-voice — proves the small-block codegen strategy actually avoids the ~8KB/voice footprint the default block-size codegen would produce, keeping many-voice polyphony affordable.

entries.key: MoogPipeline / placement-construct into a raw buffer (no heap) and run
entries.what: Placement-news a MoogLadderAdapter into a raw stack buffer (no heap), sets cutoff 500 Hz, and asserts 200 Hz RMS is finite and more than double the RMS at 5000 Hz (real LP attenuation) — then explicitly calls the destructor.
entries.why: Proves the Cmajor-generated Moog adapter genuinely supports the heap-free, in-place construction L7's hot-swap architecture requires, AND that the placement-constructed instance produces a real, correctly-shaped filter response (not just that it compiles).
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog adapter either can't be placement-constructed safely (a memory-safety risk for the in-place slot architecture) or produces a broken LP response when constructed that way — check both the construction mechanics and the resulting DSP correctness.
entries.compares: RMS at 200Hz (passband, finite) vs 5000Hz (stopband) of a MoogLadderAdapter placement-constructed into a raw stack buffer (no heap allocation), requiring the stopband RMS be less than half the passband RMS — an analytic LP contract plus a lifecycle/allocation-pattern proof, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated Moog filter can be constructed entirely in-place (no heap allocation, the pattern required for real-time-safe per-voice state) and still behaves like a genuine low-pass filter once running — the Cmajor Moog port is both allocation-safe and DSP-correct.

entries.key: MoogLadder / linear passband ~unity, far stopband strongly attenuated
entries.what: At fc=1000 Hz, resonance=0, measures 100 Hz (passband) and 8000 Hz (3 octaves above fc); asserts passband is 0.7-1.4 (near unity) and stopband is under 0.05.
entries.why: Baseline sanity for the Moog ladder's linear LP shape before any resonance/drive/mode-specific behavior is tested — Moog is the second, alongside-Huggett live filter model (ADR-0011, shipped v5.2 per followup_moog_filter_consolidation).
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog ladder's basic linear LP shape is broken — a fundamental regression that would affect every patch using Moog regardless of resonance or drive settings.
entries.compares: Steady-state amplitude well below cutoff (100Hz, fc=1000) against a fixed analytic band (0.7-1.4, ~unity), and 3 octaves above cutoff (8000Hz) against a fixed ceiling of <0.05 — an analytic passband/stopband contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Moog ladder filter's linear (zero resonance/drive) response is a genuine, well-behaved low-pass — passes bass at unity, strongly cuts far-above-cutoff content, the basic filter-shape contract every other Moog test builds on.

entries.key: MoogLadder / 24 dB/oct: one octave above fc ~ -24 dB relative to 2 octaves below
entries.what: Compares magnitude at 250 Hz (2 octaves below fc=1000) vs 2000 Hz (1 octave above); asserts the resulting slope reads -18 to -30 dB (centered on the nominal 24 dB/oct 4-pole ladder rolloff).
entries.why: Pins the authentic 4-pole ladder slope — the defining sonic characteristic that distinguishes the 24 dB Moog mode from lesser filter orders.
entries.deviationMeans: The 24 dB ladder slope has drifted outside a generous +/-6 dB tolerance band around the nominal -24 dB/oct — a filter-order regression in the db24 slope mode.
entries.compares: The dB ratio between amplitude at one octave above cutoff (2000Hz) and two octaves below (250Hz) against a fixed analytic band of -18 to -30 dB (centered on the textbook -24 dB for a 4-pole ladder), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The 24dB/oct slope setting measures as a genuine 4-pole (24dB/octave) ladder filter — the steep slope option is the authentic Moog ladder rolloff, not a mislabeled or approximate steepness.

entries.key: MoogLadder / 12 dB tap (y2)
entries.what: Same octave-slope measurement as the 24 dB case but with Slope::db12 (the y2 intermediate ladder tap); asserts the slope reads -8 to -16 dB (centered on -12 dB/oct).
entries.why: The 12 dB tap is a distinct, separately-tappable point in the 4-pole ladder cascade (2-pole equivalent) — its own slope must be correct independent of the full 24 dB output.
entries.deviationMeans: The 12 dB ladder tap's slope has drifted — a regression specific to the y2 intermediate tap, separate from the full 4-pole (24 dB) path.
entries.compares: The dB ratio between amplitude at one octave above cutoff (2000Hz) and two octaves below (250Hz) at the 12dB slope setting against a fixed analytic band of -8 to -16 dB (centered on the textbook -12 dB for a 2-pole tap), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The 12dB/oct slope setting (tapping an earlier stage of the ladder) measures as a genuine 2-pole rolloff — offering a gentler slope option that's authentically half as steep as the full 24dB ladder, not a cosmetic variant.

entries.key: MoogLadder / Q18: MoogLadder::VoiceState fits kMaxSpineStateBytes
entries.what: Runtime-asserts m.stateSize() <= kMaxSpineStateBytes; the file also carries a compile-time static_assert on sizeof(MoogLadder::VoiceState) at file scope.
entries.why: Belt-and-suspenders enforcement of Q18's governance mechanism (compile-time static_assert PLUS a runtime check) — Moog's own per-voice state must fit the shared 512-byte slot budget alongside Huggett's.
entries.deviationMeans: MoogLadder's voice state has grown past the shared per-voice slot budget — per Q18, either bump kMaxSpineStateBytes deliberately or slim the state; never let this go unnoticed.
entries.compares: MoogLadder::stateSize() against the fixed constant kMaxSpineStateBytes — a structural budget check per register question Q18, no golden file (there's also a compile-time static_assert on the same property at file scope).
entries.succeedingMeans: The Moog ladder's per-voice state fits within the fixed per-voice state memory budget shared by every filter model — Moog can't silently regress the in-place/no-heap-allocation voice architecture by growing its state too large.

entries.key: MoogLadder / resonance grows the peak at fc and thins the bass
entries.what: Compares peak-at-fc and bass (80 Hz) magnitude at resonance=0 vs 0.9; asserts the high-resonance peak exceeds 1.5x the low-resonance peak, and high-resonance bass is thinner than low-resonance bass.
entries.why: Pins the textbook Moog ladder resonance behavior — a resonant peak that grows AND a bass-thinning side-effect (the classic 1/(1+r) low-frequency loss noted in the user's Moog-filter-consolidation memory) — both must move together as authentic ladder behavior.
entries.deviationMeans: Resonance is no longer growing the peak or no longer thinning the bass — a core Moog-authenticity regression; note per user memory the bass-thinning IS considered authentic (not a bug to gain-compensate away).
entries.compares: Amplitude at the resonant peak (fc=1000Hz) and in the bass (80Hz) at low resonance (0) vs high resonance (0.9), requiring the peak grow by a factor of >1.5 and the bass amplitude decrease — a relative self-comparison across the filter's own resonance setting, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Turning up the Moog's Resonance knob genuinely emphasizes the cutoff frequency while thinning the bass below it — the classic 'ladder filter resonance' behavior (peak grows, low end recedes) is real, not just a level bump.

entries.key: MoogLadder / self-oscillation: sustains + tracks fc within 3% (7-point sweep)
entries.what: Kicks the ladder with an impulse at max resonance across 7 cutoffs (55-3520 Hz, ~6 octaves) and measures self-oscillation pitch via zero-crossing rate; asserts the measured pitch tracks fc within 3% and tail energy sustains (>1.0) at every gated point; also logs (non-gating) drift diagnostics at 5000/7000 Hz.
entries.why: Self-oscillation pitch tracking is the filter-validation-standard requirement (feedback_filter_sweep_standard: self-osc pitch within ±3% up to ~4kHz, report above) applied directly to the Moog ladder's own unit test, independent of the characterization harness.
entries.deviationMeans: The ladder's self-oscillating pitch has drifted from its cutoff by more than 3% at one of the 7 gated points, or stopped sustaining — either a tuning regression (the filter would sound out of tune when self-oscillating, e.g. played as a sine-like tone) or a resonance-loop stability regression.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-rate-derived pitch of a kicked, self-oscillating (resonance=1.0) filter at 7 cutoffs spanning ~6 octaves (55Hz-3520Hz) against the literal cutoff frequency itself, requiring <3% error and sustained tail energy (>1.0) — the filter's own cutoff setting is the analytic reference, no golden file; also logs (non-gating) diagnostic drift at 5000/7000 Hz.
entries.succeedingMeans: Maxing out resonance produces a genuine, pitched self-oscillating whistle that tracks the Cutoff knob accurately across most of the audible range — the classic 'ladder filter as an oscillator' behavior works and is in tune, at least up to ~3.5kHz (drift above that is tracked but not yet gated).

entries.key: MoogLadder / ROBUSTNESS: self-osc is loud at res=1.0 and engages over a range (not a knife-edge)
entries.what: Measures self-osc tail-peak amplitude at res=1.0 (fc 220/880 Hz, asserts peak >0.2 — 'loud') and at res=0.98 (fc 440 Hz, asserts peak >0.05 — sustains below max resonance too, not a knife-edge).
entries.why: Regression gate specifically for a resonance-TAPER calibration bug: the previous taper mapped res=1.0 to exactly the Barkhausen threshold (r=4), so self-oscillation only marginally sustained (tail peak ~0.008) and didn't engage at all below res=1.0 — an unusable knife-edge control feel.
entries.deviationMeans: The resonance taper has regressed to the knife-edge behavior — self-oscillation would only barely happen at the exact top of the knob, making the control feel broken/unpredictable to play rather than smoothly engaging over the top portion of its range.
entries.compares: Post-limiter tail-peak amplitude of a kicked filter at resonance=1.0 (two cutoffs) against a fixed floor of >0.2 (a genuinely loud, robust oscillation), and at resonance=0.98 against a fixed floor of >0.05 (still sustains just below max) — this is the regression guard for a specific resonance-taper calibration fix where res=1.0 previously mapped to exactly the Barkhausen threshold (marginal, tail peak ~0.008) with no sustain below it (a knife-edge).
entries.succeedingMeans: Cranking resonance to its maximum produces a genuinely loud, robust self-oscillation (not a barely-audible marginal buzz), and the effect engages smoothly over the top few percent of the knob's range rather than only at the exact maximum — a user reaching for 'screaming resonance' actually gets it, reliably.

entries.key: MoogLadder / bounded + finite at max res/drive/loud input
entries.what: Drives the ladder at res=1.0/drive=1.0 with a loud (4.0 amplitude) 120 Hz tone; asserts finite output and peak under 2.0 (the limiter ceiling). Separately, an impulse-kicked self-osc diagnostic at fc=440 asserts peak stays under 1.5 (CALIB_LIM_CEIL) and crest factor is 1.3-1.6 (near-sine, ~sqrt(2)=1.414).
entries.why: Pins the Pirkle-style output limiter (CALIB_LIM_CEIL=1.5) that Moog uses to internally bound its resonance — this is the exact mechanism the user's project memory contrasts with Huggett's (pre-Q27-fix) lack of an internal ceiling; the code comment notes 'without the limiter the drv=0 ladder rings far above 1.5' — the limiter is explicitly a tested feature, not incidental.
entries.deviationMeans: Either extreme drive/resonance produces non-finite/unbounded output (the internal limiter has regressed) or the self-oscillation is no longer near-sine (crest factor outside 1.3-1.6, meaning the limiter is clipping harshly rather than smoothly bounding) — Moog's whole 'tame vs Huggett' character depends on this limiter working correctly.
entries.compares: Peak output amplitude under a loud (4x), max-resonance/max-drive driven signal against a fixed ceiling of <2.0 (the output limiter's contract) plus std::isfinite, and separately the self-oscillation-only (drv=0) peak/crest-factor against fixed bands (peak <1.5, matching the documented CALIB_LIM_CEIL=1.5, and crest factor 1.3-1.6, near-sine ~1.414) — fixed safety/character thresholds, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Even the most extreme combination of resonance, drive, and input level cannot make the Moog filter blow up or exceed its designed output ceiling, and pure self-oscillation settles into a clean, near-sinusoidal limit cycle rather than a jagged one — the output limiter (an explicitly tested, not incidental, feature) genuinely bounds worst-case playing.

entries.key: MoogLadder / HP mode: low-frequency magnitude attenuated vs LP
entries.what: At fc=1000 Hz, probe 100 Hz, compares LP mode vs HP mode magnitude; asserts HP attenuates lows by more than 6 dB (factor 0.5) relative to LP.
entries.why: Confirms Moog's HP mode (part of its multi-mode tap set alongside LP/BP) genuinely rejects low frequencies rather than just being a relabeled LP output.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's HP mode is not meaningfully different from LP at low frequencies — the mode-switch dispatch for HP may be broken (see also the Layer-level Moog-mode-dispatch test in LayerTests.cpp).
entries.compares: Amplitude at 100Hz (well below the 1000Hz cutoff) in HP mode vs LP mode, requiring HP be less than half of LP (>6dB relative attenuation) — a relative cross-mode self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Selecting High-Pass mode on the Moog filter genuinely rejects bass relative to Low-Pass mode at the same cutoff — the mode selector produces a real, structurally distinct HP response, not a relabeled LP.

entries.key: MoogLadder / BP mode: mid-band probe exceeds both low and high probes
entries.what: At fc=1000 Hz, compares BP mode magnitude at 100 Hz (low), 1000 Hz (mid/centre), and 8000 Hz (high); asserts the mid-band peak exceeds both low and high by more than 3 dB (factor 1.41).
entries.why: Confirms Moog's BP mode produces a genuine band-pass shape (peak at centre, rejection on both sides), not just a relabeled LP or HP.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's BP mode no longer peaks in the middle relative to its skirts — a regression in the BP tap's combination of the ladder's LP/HP stages.
entries.compares: Amplitude at the center frequency (1000Hz) against both a low probe (100Hz) and high probe (8000Hz) in Band-Pass mode, requiring the mid-band exceed both by a factor of >1.41 (>3dB) — a relative structural self-comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Selecting Band-Pass mode genuinely produces a peak at the cutoff frequency with rejection on both sides — the BP mode has the real band-shaped response its name promises, not a mislabeled LP or HP.

entries.key: MoogLadder / setSeparation is a no-op: identical output for sep=0 vs sep=2
entries.what: Runs identical input through two fresh MoogLadder states with setSeparation(0) vs setSeparation(2); asserts the output buffers are byte-identical via memcmp on both channels.
entries.why: MoogLadder is a single-ladder model with no dual-filter separation concept (unlike Huggett's two-cell design) — setSeparation must be inert on Moog rather than silently doing something undefined, since the common-core UI (per ADR-0011) exposes it for both models.
entries.deviationMeans: Calling setSeparation on the Moog model is no longer a true no-op — the shared common-core parameter is leaking into a model that has no use for it, potentially producing an unintended or undefined coloration when a user adjusts separation while Moog is selected.
entries.compares: Byte-for-byte memcmp of output buffers rendered with setSeparation(0) vs setSeparation(2) (same cutoff/resonance otherwise) — the filter's own zero-separation output is the reference, no golden file; documents that MoogLadder is a single-ladder model with no separation analog.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Separation parameter (meaningful for the dual-cell Huggett filter) has zero effect on Moog, exactly as documented — Moog can't accidentally develop a hidden, undocumented dependency on a parameter that doesn't apply to its single-ladder topology.

entries.key: MoogLadder / Arturia golden match (skipped until data captured)
entries.what: Conditionally-run: if tests/golden/moog/response.csv exists (captured from a real Arturia Mini V per the filter-characterization harness), compares each row's cutoff/resonance/probe against the ladder's own measured dB within a 6 dB CALIB tolerance (to be tightened during calibration); otherwise logs a skip.
entries.why: This is the Q25 de-risk-order first step made concrete as a test: fingerprint the Arturia Mini V (software) FIRST to prove the whole capture->calibrate->fit method with zero rig risk, before ever facing the real Summit hardware — the test is a placeholder gate waiting for that captured data.
entries.deviationMeans: Once Arturia golden data exists, a failure here means the Moog model has drifted more than 6 dB from the captured reference at some cutoff/resonance/probe combination — until then, this test only reports whether the golden data is present at all.
entries.compares: Measured Moog response (dB) against rows of a golden CSV file (docs path via BERNIE_GOLDEN_DIR: moog/response.csv, captured from a real Arturia MiniBrute/MiniV hardware reference) at each row's fc/resonance/probe, within a currently-loose 6dB tolerance (marked CALIB: tighten during calibration) — an external golden file, but the test SKIPS entirely (logs and returns) if that file doesn't exist yet, which is the current repo state per the standing authenticity-purist hold on real-hardware fingerprinting.
entries.succeedingMeans: Nothing is verified by this test today — it is a placeholder that will compare the software Moog model against captured real Arturia hardware response once that reference data exists (the SP-D hardware fingerprinting milestone); until then this entry documents an intentional gap, not a passing claim about hardware fidelity.

entries.key: MoogLadder / bass voice adds energy at the played fundamental; amount=0 is a no-op
entries.what: With silent audio input, compares bassAmount=0 (must be bit-identical +/-0 silence, verified via fpclassify==FP_ZERO) against bassAmount=0.8 at fundamental 110 Hz (must produce energy concentrated near 110 Hz, verified by zero-crossing pitch within 5%).
entries.why: The Moog sub-osc bass-boost feature (per user project memory: 'opt-in sub-osc bass, not auto-makeup') must be a true no-op when disabled and must actually track the played note's fundamental when enabled — this is the Layer/Voice-level setVoiceContext hookup's payoff.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the bass voice leaks energy into silence when it should be a no-op (amount=0), or the bass voice at nonzero amount doesn't track the played pitch — both would break the opt-in, authenticity-preserving design intent for the sub-osc feature.
entries.compares: Output on fully-silent input at bassAmount=0 against exact zero (fpclassify==FP_ZERO, bit-exact no-op), and at bassAmount=0.8 the energy against a fixed floor (>1.0) plus zero-crossing-derived pitch against the literal fundamental 110Hz (within 5%) — analytic/exact checks, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Moog's sub-oscillator bass-voice feature is genuinely silent when its amount is zero (no hidden bleed), and when turned on it adds real, correctly-pitched energy at the note's fundamental — the authenticity-preserving 'opt-in bass, not auto-makeup' design (the standing decision from the Moog gain-comp investigation) actually holds at amount=0 and actually works at amount>0.

entries.key: MoogLadder / bassAmount=0 is bit-identical to the ladder-only path on NON-silent input
entries.what: Drives a nonlinear-path config (res=0.7, drive=0.5) with a real 220 Hz tone; compares a ladder-only instance against one with setBass(0.0, saw, 0) + setFundamental(110) configured (but amount=0); asserts byte-identical output via memcmp.
entries.why: Strengthens the silent-input no-op test by proving amount=0 is a no-op even with a NON-trivial wave/fundamental configured and non-silent, nonlinear-path input — ruling out any interaction between the bass-voice config state and the main ladder's nonlinear processing.
entries.deviationMeans: Configuring (but not enabling) the bass voice is perturbing the ladder's nonlinear output even at amount=0 — a subtler no-op violation than the silent-input case, meaning simply touching the bass-voice controls (without raising the amount) could audibly change existing patches.
entries.compares: Byte-for-byte memcmp of ladder output with the bass voice never configured vs configured with amount=0 (but a non-trivial wave/fundamental that WOULD sound if amount were nonzero), driven through the nonlinear path (resonance>0, drive>0) on a real musical tone — the untouched ladder-only path is the reference, no golden file. Stronger than the silent-input no-op test above.
entries.succeedingMeans: Even under a driven, resonant, musically-realistic signal, leaving the bass voice's amount at zero has ZERO effect on the ladder's output, down to the last bit — a patch that hasn't opted into sub-osc bass gets a filter that's provably unaffected by the bass-voice code path even existing, not just 'quiet enough.'

entries.key: MoogLadder / slope is meaningful in BP and HP (BP24 steeper than BP12; HP12 gentler than HP24)
entries.what: Compares skirt steepness at 250 Hz for BP12 vs BP24 (asserts BP24 more than 4 dB steeper) and at 500 Hz for HP12 vs HP24 (asserts HP12 more than 4 dB gentler than HP24).
entries.why: The 12/24 dB slope selector must meaningfully affect skirt steepness in EVERY mode (not just the default LP), since the UI exposes slope as a single common-core control across all modes.
entries.deviationMeans: The slope parameter has stopped differentiating BP or HP skirt steepness — a mode-specific slope-dispatch regression, even if LP's own slope behavior (tested separately) still works.
entries.compares: Skirt attenuation (dB) two octaves below center in BP mode comparing 12dB vs 24dB slope (requiring BP24 < BP12-4dB), and one octave below cutoff in HP mode comparing 12dB vs 24dB slope (requiring HP12 > HP24+4dB) — relative self-comparisons between the filter's own slope settings in non-LP modes, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Slope selector (12dB vs 24dB) has a real, measurable effect not just in Low-Pass mode but also in Band-Pass and High-Pass mode — choosing a gentler or steeper slope genuinely changes the sound regardless of which filter mode is active.

entries.key: MoogLadder / setVoiceContext drives the sub-osc fundamental (base no-op; Moog override)
entries.what: Configures a bass voice (amount=0.8) then calls setVoiceContext(state, 110.0f) — the polymorphic per-voice-context hook — and asserts it produces energy (the sub-osc engaging at the given fundamental); also calls the same hook through the base FilterModel& reference at a different pitch and asserts it compiles/runs safely as a no-op for models that don't override it.
entries.why: setVoiceContext is a FilterModel-interface polymorphic hook (per the register's mention of 'setVoiceContext sub-osc' in the Moog consolidation) — Moog is the one model that meaningfully overrides it (to drive the bass voice's fundamental), while the base class default must remain safely inert for models like Huggett that don't use it.
entries.deviationMeans: Either Moog's override no longer drives the sub-osc fundamental (breaking the bass-voice pitch-tracking feature) or the base-class no-op default crashes/misbehaves when called on a model that doesn't override it (a polymorphic-interface safety regression affecting every model, not just Moog).
entries.compares: Energy of silent-input output after calling setVoiceContext(110.0) (which should drive the sub-osc fundamental via the polymorphic FilterModel hook) against a fixed floor of >1.0, plus confirms the base FilterModel::setVoiceContext (called through a base-class reference) compiles and doesn't crash as a no-op — a virtual-dispatch/override contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The generic per-voice-context hook (used to tell any filter model what note is playing) correctly reaches and drives Moog's sub-oscillator fundamental when Moog overrides it, while remaining a harmless no-op for filter models (like the base class) that don't care about voice context — the polymorphic architecture lets Moog opt into note-awareness without other models needing to know or care.

entries.key: MoogMaxFreq / Moog codegen max-frequency cap raised for oversampling
entries.what: Asserts the generated MoogLadderAdapter's getMaxFrequency() is at least 768000 Hz (covering 8x oversampling at 96k), and that prepare(384000) (8x@48k) and prepare(768000) (8x@96k) both complete without assertion/throw.
entries.why: The Cmajor-generated Moog codegen has an internal max-frequency cap that must be explicitly raised to cover the OS8 tier at both common host rates — this guards against the cap silently reverting to a value too low for the 64-voices-at-os8 product target (Q2).
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog codegen's frequency cap has reverted below what os8 oversampling needs — preparing a Moog-model voice at the highest OS tier would jassert/throw or silently misbehave, breaking the Q2 64-voices-at-every-OS-tier guarantee specifically for Moog.
entries.compares: MoogLadderAdapter::getMaxFrequency() against a fixed floor of >=768000.0 Hz (8x oversampling at 96kHz base rate), plus prepare() at 384kHz and 768kHz completing without assertion — a fixed structural ceiling check, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The Cmajor-generated Moog filter's internal frequency-cap codegen parameter has been raised high enough to actually run at the highest oversampling tier (8x) at either supported base sample rate — the Moog Cmajor port isn't silently capped below what the product's oversampling range requires.

entries.key: OversamplingAntiAlias / 4x oversampling reduces alias energy of a driven Huggett filter
entries.what: Renders a hot-driven Huggett voice (saw, res=0.95, full spine drive + post-drive, MIDI 88/~1318.5 Hz) at OS factor 1 vs 4, computes the amplitude-independent alias FRACTION (inharmonic/total spectral energy) for each, and asserts frac4 < frac1*0.7 (>=1.4x reduction); measured frac1~=0.257, frac4~=0.014 (~18x) post-Q27-fix.
entries.why: End-to-end proof that raising OS factor genuinely reduces aliasing on a full, real, hot-driven voice (not a synthetic device like CharacterizationRunnerTests' version) — explicitly re-anchored after the Q27 fix since the old anchor relied on the pre-fix expansive scream being ~54% aliasing by itself.
entries.deviationMeans: Oversampling a fully-driven real voice is no longer meaningfully reducing aliasing — note the test explicitly scopes itself to the oversampling INTEGRATION and alias fraction, not halfband stopband depth (that's Halfband2xTests' job), so a failure here points at the OS-path wiring, not the resampler filter itself.
entries.compares: The alias-energy FRACTION (inharmonic spectral energy / total energy, amplitude-independent) of a hot-driven Huggett filter rendered at oversampling factor 1 vs factor 4, requiring frac4 < frac1*0.7 (>=1.4x reduction) — re-anchored post-Q27-fix measured values (frac1~=0.257, frac4~=0.014, ~18x actual reduction) used to set a conservative 1.4x gate; a relative self-comparison across the engine's own OS setting, no golden file. Explicitly scoped: proves the OS INTEGRATION reduces aliasing at the full Layer/Voice level, not the halfband filter's stopband depth in isolation (that's Halfband2xTests' job).
entries.succeedingMeans: Turning on 4x oversampling for a hot, driven, near-self-oscillating patch measurably and substantially reduces audible aliasing at the full-engine integration level — a user who cranks up the OS setting on an aggressive patch actually gets the cleaner sound oversampling promises, not just a per-component filter spec that never reaches the real signal path.

entries.key: MethodAgreement / maxMagDeltaDb is 0 for identical curves, exact for a known offset
entries.what: Asserts maxMagDeltaDb returns exactly 0 for two identical magnitude-curve vectors, and exactly 0.5 after uniformly offsetting one curve by -0.5 dB.
entries.why: maxMagDeltaDb is the core primitive behind every dual-method (stepped-sine vs ESS) agreement gate in the suite (CharacterizationGateTests, CharacterizationRunnerTests); this is its own unit-level correctness proof before it's trusted as a gate.
entries.deviationMeans: The fundamental max-delta primitive is miscalculating — every method-agreement gate built on it (which is most of the filter-validation trust chain) would be reporting wrong numbers.
entries.compares: testdsp::MethodAgreement::maxMagDeltaDb() on two identical magnitude-dB curves against the literal value 0.0 (exact), and on a curve offset by a known constant (-0.5) against the literal value 0.5 (within 1e-9) — an exact analytic self-test of the dual-method-agreement metric, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared cross-method disagreement metric (used to compare independent measurement techniques against each other throughout the suite) computes the correct maximum delta — every 'method A agrees with method B' claim elsewhere in the suite is measured by a metric proven arithmetically exact.

entries.key: MethodAgreement / stepped-sine and ESS agree on a synthetic LP (the dual-method gate)
entries.what: Measures a synthetic Cytomic-style LP cell (fc=1000, res=0) via both stepped-sine and ESS (calibrated against the identity reference to remove the sweep's constant gain offset) across 60 log-spaced probes 50-20kHz; asserts max magnitude delta between the two methods is under 1.0 dB.
entries.why: This is the exact dual-method gate mechanism the whole filter-validation harness trust model depends on — proven here on a synthetic, perfectly-known LP before ever being applied to a real (Huggett/Moog) filter.
entries.deviationMeans: The two independent measurement methods disagree on even a synthetic, perfectly-known filter — the entire dual-method trust chain (used everywhere real filters are characterized) is undermined at its foundation.
entries.compares: Maximum magnitude-dB delta (via maxMagDeltaDb) between the stepped-sine method and the ESS (exponential sine sweep) method measuring the SAME synthetic linear LP filter across 60 log-spaced frequencies (50Hz-20kHz), requiring delta <1.0 dB — cross-method agreement between two independently-implemented measurement techniques, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Two completely independent ways of measuring a filter's frequency response (stepped-sine and sweep-deconvolution) agree with each other on a known filter — this dual-method cross-check is the core trust mechanism behind every characterization-battery measurement in the suite: if a real filter under test ever showed the two methods disagreeing, that would flag a measurement-tooling problem rather than a real filter defect.

entries.key: MethodAgreement / Harmonics::thdDb ~ floor for a clean passthrough
entries.what: Measures THD of a no-op Passthrough device and asserts it reads below -60 dB (essentially the measurement noise floor).
entries.why: Baseline sanity that the THD measurement tool itself doesn't manufacture distortion out of a perfectly clean signal — a prerequisite for trusting any THD number measured on a real filter.
entries.deviationMeans: The THD measurement tool is reporting spurious distortion on a signal that has none — every THD reading elsewhere in the suite becomes suspect.
entries.compares: testdsp::Harmonics::thdDb() of a genuine no-op passthrough processor against a fixed noise-floor ceiling of <-60 dB — a self-test of the THD-measurement helper on a known-clean system, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared THD (total harmonic distortion) measurement helper correctly reads near-zero distortion for a system that adds none — distortion numbers reported elsewhere in the suite via this specific helper reflect the device under test, not measurement-tool artifacts.

entries.key: MethodAgreement / EssResponse magnitude is delay-invariant (a pure delay reads ~0 dB all-pass)
entries.what: Measures a pure D=30-sample delay device via EssResponse across 40 probes 300-6000 Hz; asserts the magnitude reads within 0.5 dB of 0 dB (all-pass) everywhere in that band — proving magnitude is delay-invariant even though phase/group-delay from the same measurement are NOT (Q20's known limitation: the deconvolved IR's bulk latency wraps phase ~9x off before time-alignment).
entries.why: This is the specific test that surfaced Q20 (2026-06-30): it PROVES magnitude stays trustworthy for delay-affected signals (justifying every magnitude-only gate in the suite) while simultaneously demonstrating why phase/group-delay from EssResponse are currently descriptive-only until the IR time-alignment fix lands.
entries.deviationMeans: If this regresses, the magnitude-only trust model itself (which every gate in the suite relies on) is broken — this is one of the most foundational tests in the harness; do not confuse with a phase/group-delay issue, which is the separately-tracked, already-known Q20 limitation.
entries.compares: EssResponse-measured magnitude (dB) of a pure 30-sample delay processor, at frequencies 500-4000Hz, against the literal analytic expectation 0 dB (all-pass, within 0.5 dB) — an exact theoretical property (a pure delay has flat unity magnitude response at every frequency), no golden file. Explicitly documents a KNOWN LIMITATION: this same method's phase/group-delay output is NOT yet trustworthy (a tracked, unfixed IR time-alignment issue causes ~9x error on the same delay) — no gate anywhere depends on ESS phase/group-delay, only magnitude.
entries.succeedingMeans: The ESS (exponential sine sweep) measurement method's magnitude output is correctly calibrated to be independent of a filter's bulk time delay — the sweep-based magnitude measurements trusted throughout the suite are sound, even though this same file is honest that the ESS phase/group-delay columns are a known, tracked, not-yet-fixed limitation that nothing currently depends on.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M1 finite @drive=0.0
entries.what: At drive=0.0, processes a bin-aligned 8192-sample tone through AsymSaturator and gates that every output sample is finite.
entries.why: M1 — baseline finiteness gate for the shaper across its drive range; drive=0.0 is the 'off' reference point of the 3-point sweep (0.0, 0.5, 1.0).
entries.deviationMeans: The shaper produces non-finite output even at drive=0 (its most inert setting) — a fundamental regression that would affect every patch since drive=0 should be the safest possible configuration.
entries.compares: Every output sample of the AsymSaturator at drive=0.0 against std::isfinite (testdsp::Metrics::finite) — a basic stability gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The production drive/shaper stage produces clean, finite output at its lowest drive setting — the harness's foundational sanity check before the harder aliasing/THD measurements below it are trusted.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M4 NSR v5.0-regression @drive=0.0
entries.what: Compares a base-rate-processed shaper output against a 32x-oversampled-then-decimated 'truth' reference at drive=0.0, computing noise-to-signal ratio (aliasing); gates against a v5.0-measured baseline (-26.82 dB) + 3 dB headroom (-23.0 dB gate).
entries.why: M4 — a frozen v5.0 aliasing baseline explicitly NOT targeting the literature ideal (<-60dB for 32x-OS-referenced clean nonlinearities); the code comment notes ADAA was tried and measured no better, so this gate exists purely to catch REGRESSION (more aliasing) from the accepted baseline, not to claim the aliasing is already good.
entries.deviationMeans: Aliasing at drive=0.0 has worsened beyond the frozen v5.0 baseline — since the baseline itself is already accepted-as-imperfect (not a target for improvement), a regression here means something made an already-known-suboptimal situation actively worse.
entries.compares: Noise-to-signal ratio (NSR, aliasing) of the base-rate shaper at drive=0.0 against a fixed gate of -23.0 dB (measured baseline -26.82 dB + 3 dB headroom, committed 2026-06-20), compared against a 32x-oversampled-then-decimated reference as ground truth — a frozen regression anchor, not the theoretical <-60 dB literature target (plain base-rate tanh aliases more than that; ADAA was tried and measured no improvement).
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's aliasing at its lowest drive setting has not gotten WORSE than the measured v5.0 baseline — this doesn't claim the aliasing is inaudible (it isn't, by literature standards, at base rate), only that it hasn't silently regressed further from the accepted, already-imperfect baseline.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M6 THD+N golden @drive=0.0
entries.what: Computes THD+N of the drive=0.0 shaper output and golden-checks it against a committed baseline (tests/golden 'shaper_thd' set) within 1 dB.
entries.why: M6 — a golden-regression companion to M4's fixed-threshold NSR gate, catching ANY drift in the distortion measurement at drive=0 even if it stays within M4's aliasing tolerance.
entries.deviationMeans: THD+N at drive=0.0 has drifted from its golden baseline — investigate whether this is a benign numeric drift or an actual character change to the shaper's 'off' setting; per the goldens convention, an intentional change regenerates via BERNIE_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 with a justified commit message.
entries.compares: THD+N (dB) of the base-rate shaper at drive=0.0 against a committed golden value in tests/golden/shaper_thd.csv (key thd_drive_0.0, value -22.656725 dB), within +-1.0 dB via testdsp::GoldenSet — an external golden CSV file, the exact worked-example pattern.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's harmonic distortion character at zero drive is pinned to a specific, previously-measured-and-approved number — any change (intentional retuning or accidental regression) to the shaper's distortion behavior at this setting will be caught by drifting off this golden value.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M1 finite @drive=0.5
entries.what: Same M1 finiteness gate as the drive=0.0 case, at drive=0.5 (the mid-point of the 3-point sweep).
entries.why: Confirms finiteness holds at a moderate, musically-common drive setting, not just the extremes.
entries.deviationMeans: The shaper produces non-finite output at a moderate drive setting — a stability regression in the middle of the shaper's normal operating range.
entries.compares: Every output sample of the AsymSaturator at drive=0.5 against std::isfinite — a basic stability gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The production drive/shaper stage produces clean, finite output at a moderate drive setting — stability holds as the user turns the Drive knob up from its minimum.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M4 NSR v5.0-regression @drive=0.5
entries.what: Same NSR-vs-oversampled-truth measurement at drive=0.5; gates against the v5.0-measured baseline (-12.44 dB) + 3 dB headroom (-9.0 dB gate).
entries.why: Mid-drive point of the same frozen-baseline regression-only gate as the drive=0.0 case — aliasing naturally increases with drive (measured -26.82 to -8.31 dB across the sweep), so each drive level has its own separately-anchored gate rather than one shared threshold.
entries.deviationMeans: Aliasing at moderate drive has worsened beyond its own frozen baseline — since aliasing legitimately increases with drive by design, compare the delta against the OTHER two drive points' gates to see if the whole curve shifted (broader regression) or just this point.
entries.compares: NSR (dB) of the base-rate shaper at drive=0.5 against a fixed gate of -9.0 dB (measured baseline -12.44 dB + 3 dB headroom, committed 2026-06-20) — a frozen regression anchor, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's aliasing at a moderate drive setting has not regressed beyond the accepted v5.0 baseline — moderate-drive playing isn't getting audibly buzzier over time due to an unnoticed code change.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M6 THD+N golden @drive=0.5
entries.what: THD+N golden check at drive=0.5 against the committed 'shaper_thd' baseline, within 1 dB.
entries.why: Mid-drive point of the M6 golden-regression sweep, complementing M4's fixed-threshold gate at the same drive level.
entries.deviationMeans: THD+N at moderate drive has drifted from its golden — check whether this correlates with the M4 NSR gate at the same drive level also moving, which would suggest a shared root cause.
entries.compares: THD+N (dB) of the base-rate shaper at drive=0.5 against the committed golden value in tests/golden/shaper_thd.csv (key thd_drive_0.5, value -10.932786 dB), within +-1.0 dB — an external golden CSV file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's harmonic distortion character at moderate drive is pinned to a specific approved number — the mid-drive coloration a user actually plays with most is protected against silent drift.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M1 finite @drive=1.0
entries.what: Same M1 finiteness gate at drive=1.0 (maximum drive).
entries.why: Confirms finiteness at the shaper's most extreme, highest-drive setting — the configuration most likely to expose a stability bug if one exists.
entries.deviationMeans: The shaper produces non-finite output at maximum drive — the most extreme (and thus highest-risk) configuration has a stability regression.
entries.compares: Every output sample of the AsymSaturator at drive=1.0 (maximum) against std::isfinite — a basic stability gate, no golden file.
