entries.succeedingMeans: The production drive/shaper stage stays numerically stable at its maximum, most-aggressive drive setting — cranking Drive to its limit can't produce NaN/Inf garbage.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M4 NSR v5.0-regression @drive=1.0
entries.what: Same NSR-vs-oversampled-truth measurement at drive=1.0; gates against the v5.0-measured baseline (-8.31 dB) + 3 dB headroom (-5.0 dB gate) — the loosest (most aliasing-tolerant) gate of the three, since maximum drive is expected to alias the most.
entries.why: Maximum-drive point of the frozen-baseline regression gate — completes the 3-point sweep anchoring aliasing behavior across the shaper's full drive range.
entries.deviationMeans: Aliasing at maximum drive has worsened beyond its already-loose baseline — since this is already the most aliasing-tolerant gate in the sweep, a failure here represents a larger absolute increase in unwanted aliasing than an equivalent-margin failure at lower drive.
entries.compares: NSR (dB) of the base-rate shaper at drive=1.0 against a fixed gate of -5.0 dB (measured baseline -8.31 dB + 3 dB headroom, committed 2026-06-20) — a frozen regression anchor, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's aliasing at maximum drive (its worst-case, most alias-prone setting) has not regressed beyond the accepted v5.0 baseline — the most aggressive drive setting isn't getting silently buzzier.

entries.key: SpineShaperHarness / SpineShaper M6 THD+N golden @drive=1.0
entries.what: THD+N golden check at drive=1.0 (maximum drive) against the committed baseline, within 1 dB.
entries.why: Completes the M6 golden-regression sweep across all three drive points, anchoring the shaper's maximum-drive distortion character.
entries.deviationMeans: THD+N at maximum drive has drifted from its golden — the shaper's most extreme, most audibly 'driven' character has changed; check whether M4's NSR gate at the same drive point also shifted.
entries.compares: THD+N (dB) of the base-rate shaper at drive=1.0 against the committed golden value in tests/golden/shaper_thd.csv (key thd_drive_1.0, value -7.366037 dB), within +-1.0 dB — an external golden CSV file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shaper's harmonic distortion character at maximum drive is pinned to a specific approved number — the most extreme, character-defining drive setting is protected against silent drift.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M1 finite (post-drive 1.0, amp 2.0)
entries.what: Processes a hot (amp=2.0) sine through HuggettFilter's post-drive path (postDrive=1.0, blocks of 128) and gates that the output is entirely finite via testdsp::Metrics::finite.
entries.why: M1 in the acceptance-criterion metric numbering — the baseline finiteness gate for the full post-drive path under a genuinely hot, fully-driven signal, complementing the plain-expect versions in HuggettNonlinearTests/OverdriveDiagnosticTests.
entries.deviationMeans: The post-drive path is producing non-finite output under a hot, fully-driven signal — a serious DSP stability regression in the exact configuration (high amp, full drive) most likely to expose one.
entries.compares: Every output sample of HuggettFilter (post-drive maxed, amp 2.0, block-processed) against std::isfinite via testdsp::Metrics::finite — a basic stability gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The production Huggett filter's post-drive path stays numerically clean under a hot, maximally-driven signal in the harness's standard operating point — the foundational sanity check the harder M2/M8/M13 gates below build on.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M2 maxAbs <= 4.0 (post-drive 1.0, amp 2.0)
entries.what: On the same hot post-drive signal, gates the peak absolute output at <= 4.0 via testdsp::Gate.
entries.why: M2 — bounded-output guarantee for the post-drive stage, the same class of safety property Q27 fixed for the resonance loop, applied here to the drive (not resonance) nonlinearity.
entries.deviationMeans: The post-drive stage's output is exceeding the bounded ceiling under a hot signal — a stability regression in the drive nonlinearity, distinct from (but analogous to) the Q27 resonance-loop defect.
entries.compares: Peak absolute output sample of a hot post-drive render against a fixed ceiling of 4.0 via testdsp::Gate — a safety/boundedness contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Even with post-drive maxed and a loud input, the Huggett filter's output stays within a bounded, safe range — a hearing-safety-adjacent regression guard on the drive stage's worst case.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M8 block-vs-sample <= 1e-5 (blockSize=64)
entries.what: Processes the same hot post-drive signal in 64-sample blocks vs sample-at-a-time (blockSize=1) and gates the max per-sample difference at <= 1e-5.
entries.why: M8 is the regression guard for a specific fixed bug: the code comment documents a v5.1.0 fix that removed a 'g_eff droop click' — the droop is now correctly gated on drive/resonance so the linear path is linear-by-construction, meaning block-vs-sample processing must be bit-identical (within float rounding) at every block size.
entries.deviationMeans: Processing in 64-sample blocks now differs measurably from per-sample processing — the g_eff droop click bug (fixed in v5.1.0) may have regressed, which would mean the plugin's actual block-size (128-1024 typical) affects the sound differently than expected.
entries.compares: Max per-sample difference between the post-drive path processed in 64-sample blocks vs one-sample-at-a-time, against a fixed ceiling of 1e-5 — this IS the regression guard for the g_eff droop click bug fixed in v5.1.0 (droop is now gated on drive/resonance so the linear path is block-invariant), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Processing audio in realistic 64-sample host blocks produces bit-identical results to per-sample processing — the historical bug where per-block coefficient recalculation caused an audible click/droop cannot recur at this block size.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M8 block-vs-sample <= 1e-5 (blockSize=128)
entries.what: Same M8 block-vs-sample equivalence check as the 64-sample case, at blockSize=128 (a common real host buffer size).
entries.why: 128 samples is a realistic DAW block size — this specific instance of the M8 gate is the one closest to real-world host conditions, not just a parametric sweep point.
entries.deviationMeans: At a realistic host block size (128), the droop-click regression would be directly audible in normal use, not just a theoretical edge case.
entries.compares: Max per-sample difference between the post-drive path processed in 128-sample blocks vs one-sample-at-a-time, against a fixed ceiling of 1e-5 — the same g_eff droop regression guard, checked at a second common host block size.
entries.succeedingMeans: Processing audio in 128-sample host blocks (a very common DAW buffer size) produces bit-identical results to per-sample processing — the block-size-dependent droop click cannot recur at this size either.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M8 block-vs-sample <= 1e-5 (blockSize=256)
entries.what: Same M8 block-vs-sample equivalence check at blockSize=256.
entries.why: Extends the g_eff droop-click regression guard to a third common block size, confirming the block-independence property holds across the range of buffer sizes a host might use.
entries.deviationMeans: The droop-click regression is block-size-dependent — comparing which of the three tested block sizes (64/128/256) fail would help isolate whether the bug is a fixed-size threshold effect or a general regression.
entries.compares: Max per-sample difference between the post-drive path processed in 256-sample blocks vs one-sample-at-a-time, against a fixed ceiling of 1e-5 — the same g_eff droop regression guard, checked at a third common host block size.
entries.succeedingMeans: Processing audio in 256-sample host blocks produces bit-identical results to per-sample processing — the block-invariance guarantee holds across the range of block sizes a real host might use.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett M13 zero-drive == bare NlSvfCell <= 1e-5
entries.what: At cutoff 1200 Hz, db12, zero drive/resonance, compares HuggettFilter sample-by-sample against a standalone NlSvfCell configured identically; gates max difference at <= 1e-5.
entries.why: M13 — the gated-harness port of HuggettNonlinearTests' 'zero drive/resonance == bare linear cell' check (explicitly noted in the file header as mirroring that test), proving the linear no-op guarantee holds under the formal Gate convention too.
entries.deviationMeans: HuggettFilter's zero-drive linear degeneracy has broken against the M13 gate — cross-check HuggettNonlinearTests' equivalent (non-gated) test to see if this is a shared DSP regression or specific to this harness.
entries.compares: Per-sample output of the full HuggettFilter (drive/resonance both zero, db12) against a bare reference NlSvfCell at the same cutoff, requiring max diff <=1e-5 — cross-method/reference-implementation agreement, no golden file; mirrors the equivalent check in HuggettNonlinearTests as a harness-level regression gate.
entries.succeedingMeans: With all Huggett-specific nonlinear machinery neutralized, the harness's own gated regression suite confirms the filter is bit-for-bit identical to the plain linear filter core — this specific check is committed as a standing regression gate, not just a one-off unit test, so it can't silently break without the harness catching it.

entries.key: SpineHuggettHarness / SpineHuggett stereo L==R correlation >= 0.999
entries.what: Feeds a mono sine as identical stereo input (amp 0.9, postDrive 0.5) through HuggettFilter and gates L/R output correlation at >= 0.999.
entries.why: The spine filter uses mono coefficients (documented in the file header) — a mono-in signal fed to both channels must produce virtually identical L/R output, since any per-channel divergence would mean unwanted stereo decorrelation from a supposedly mono-coefficient filter.
entries.deviationMeans: The Huggett filter is introducing L/R decorrelation on identical stereo input — since the filter is documented as using mono coefficients, any decorrelation here would be an unintended (and likely subtle/hard-to-notice) stereo-image artifact.
entries.compares: Stereo correlation (testdsp::Metrics::stereoCorrelation) between L and R channel output, given identical mono input fed to both, against a fixed floor of >=0.999 — a structural contract reflecting that the spine filter uses mono (shared) coefficients, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Feeding the same signal to both channels produces virtually identical L/R output — the spine filter's mono-coefficient design doesn't introduce any accidental stereo divergence or imaging artifact when there's no reason for one.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M1 finite (max res, 0.5 s)
entries.what: Kicks an NlSvfCell at fc=800/res=1.0/resSat=1.0 with an impulse and asserts the output stays finite through a 0.5s settling window.
entries.why: M1 — the baseline finiteness gate for NlSvfCell's raw self-oscillating state, one level below the higher-level HuggettFilter/HpStage harnesses that build on this cell.
entries.deviationMeans: The core NlSvfCell primitive itself is producing non-finite output at max resonance — since every higher-level filter (HuggettFilter, HuggettHpStage) is built on this cell, a regression here would likely cascade into all of them.
entries.compares: Peak output after a kicked impulse plus 0.5s of silence at maximum resonance/res-saturation against std::isfinite — a basic stability gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The nonlinear SVF cell stays numerically clean under a sustained self-oscillating condition — the harness's foundational sanity check before the harder shape/pitch gates below it are trusted.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M2 self-osc bounded <= 4.0
entries.what: Same kick-and-measure setup as M1, gating the settled peak at <= 4.0 via testdsp::Gate.
entries.why: M2 — the raw-cell-level bounded-resonance guarantee (the Q27 fix's most fundamental target), one level below HuggettBoundedResonanceTests' more extensive coverage.
entries.deviationMeans: The most basic self-oscillation bound at the NlSvfCell level has regressed — this is the Q27 defect's root-cause level; treat as a hearing-safety-relevant regression.
entries.compares: Peak post-settling output amplitude of a kicked cell at max resonance against a fixed ceiling of 4.0 via testdsp::Gate — a safety/boundedness contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The nonlinear SVF cell's self-oscillation stays within a bounded, safe range as a standing regression gate — this specific safety guarantee can't silently regress unnoticed.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M10 LP passband vs analytic (res=0, fc=1000 Hz, f<=fc)
entries.what: At res=0 (k=2.0, Q=0.5), gates measured LP magnitude at 100/300/500/700/1000 Hz against the exact analytic 2-pole LP formula, each within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: M10 — a formally-gated version of the same LP-vs-analytic proof TestDspSelfTests and SteppedSineTests perform, specifically isolating the LP tap of NlSvfCell under the acceptance-criterion metric numbering.
entries.deviationMeans: NlSvfCell's LP tap no longer matches the analytic 2-pole formula in its own passband — cross-check against TestDspSelfTests' equivalent Response::magDb self-test to see if the regression is cell-wide or specific to this measurement path.
entries.compares: Measured LP magnitude (dB) at 5 frequencies up to cutoff against the closed-form Cytomic-style 2-pole analytic formula -10*log10((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2) with k derived from the cell's own Q=0.5+res^2*49.5 mapping, each gated at 0.5dB via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The nonlinear SVF's LP passband shape (below cutoff) matches textbook analog 2-pole filter theory to within half a dB, as a standing harness regression gate — the LP shape can't silently drift from correct filter theory.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M10 HP passband vs analytic (res=0, fc=1000 Hz, f>=fc)
entries.what: At res=0, gates measured HP magnitude at 1000/2000/4000 Hz against the analytic HP formula (LP formula + 20*log10(u^2)) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: M10 HP variant — proves the HP tap specifically (not just LP) matches its analytic prediction; the HP formula is derived by adding a frequency-dependent term to the LP formula, so this also indirectly cross-checks the LP result.
entries.deviationMeans: NlSvfCell's HP tap has drifted from its analytic prediction — since the HP formula builds on the LP formula, check whether the LP test (M10 LP) also fails to determine if the bug is HP-specific or shared.
entries.compares: Measured HP magnitude (dB) at 3 frequencies at/above cutoff against the closed-form analytic HP formula (LP formula + 20*log10(u^2)), each gated at 0.5dB via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The nonlinear SVF's HP tap shape (above cutoff) matches textbook analog filter theory to within half a dB, as a standing harness regression gate — the HP tap, not just LP, is verified against first-principles math.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M10 BP passband vs analytic (res=0, fc=1000 Hz)
entries.what: At res=0, gates measured BP magnitude at 500/700/1000/1500/2000 Hz against the analytic BP formula (H_bp=u/sqrt((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2)) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: M10 BP variant — completes the LP/HP/BP analytic-match trio for NlSvfCell's three taps, each independently gated.
entries.deviationMeans: NlSvfCell's BP tap has drifted from its analytic prediction — if LP and HP both pass but BP fails, the bug is isolated to the band-pass tap combination logic specifically.
entries.compares: Measured BP magnitude (dB) at 5 frequencies near cutoff against the closed-form analytic BP formula H_bp(u)=u/sqrt((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2), each gated at 0.5dB via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The nonlinear SVF's BP tap shape near cutoff matches textbook analog filter theory to within half a dB, as a standing harness regression gate — all three taps (LP/HP/BP) of the shared multi-mode cell are independently verified against first-principles math.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M11 resonant peak freq +-2%, height +-1.5 dB
entries.what: At res=0.5 (Q=12.875, small-signal amp 0.005), scans 30 points 850-1150 Hz to find the measured LP resonant peak, then gates its frequency within 2% of the analytic peak (uPeak=sqrt(1-k^2/2), ~998.5 Hz) and height within 1.5 dB of the analytic peak height (~22.20 dB, baseline measured 2026-06-20).
entries.why: M11 — proves the resonant peak's frequency AND height both match closed-form predictions at a real (non-trivial) resonance setting, not just that a peak exists somewhere near the cutoff.
entries.deviationMeans: The resonant peak has shifted in frequency or changed in height beyond tolerance from its analytic prediction — a Q-mapping or resonance-gain regression at a moderate (musically-relevant) resonance setting, distinct from the max-resonance self-osc tests.
entries.compares: A fine-grid-scanned (30-point, 850-1150Hz) measured LP resonant peak (frequency and height, resonance=0.5, small-signal linear regime) against the closed-form analytic peak location u_peak=sqrt(1-k^2/2) and height H_peak=1/(k*sqrt(1-k^2/4)) (baseline measured 2026-06-20: f~=998.5Hz, H~=22.20dB), gated at 2% frequency / 1.5dB height via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The resonant peak's exact location and height at a moderate resonance setting match textbook analog filter theory precisely — resonance isn't just 'louder near cutoff', it's the mathematically correct peak, in the right place, at the right height.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=200 sr=44100
entries.what: Kicks the BP output into self-oscillation at fc=200 Hz/44100 Hz sample rate, measures pitch via zero-crossing after discarding 0.1s, and gates the error at <= 0.75% (fc<=4kHz gate) per the ratified v5 self-oscillation-pitch standard.
entries.why: Implements the exact filter-validation-standard requirement (feedback_filter_sweep_standard: self-osc pitch ±3% only up to ~4kHz gated, report above) at one specific cutoff/sample-rate combination — part of a 3-cutoff x 3-sample-rate matrix (9 total) covering the standard's full test grid.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation at 200 Hz/44.1kHz is mistuned beyond the ratified standard's gate — a played note using self-oscillation as a tone source at this pitch/rate combination would sound audibly out of tune.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation (max resonance, kicked, 0.5s collected after 0.1s discard) against the literal cutoff 200Hz, gated at the ratified v5 standard of +-0.75% for fc<=4kHz via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the analytic reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a low cutoff (200Hz) and the CD-standard 44.1kHz sample rate tracks the Cutoff knob accurately to within a fraction of a percent — the 'filter as oscillator' behavior is in tune at this common sample rate and register.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=200 sr=48000
entries.what: Same M12 self-osc pitch gate as the 44100 Hz case, at fc=200 Hz/48000 Hz sample rate.
entries.why: 48 kHz is the plugin's primary/default sample rate — this specific point in the M12 grid is the most representative of typical playing conditions.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation pitch is mistuned at the most commonly-used sample rate — the highest-priority instance of an M12 regression to fix first.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 200Hz, 48kHz sample rate, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-0.75% via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a low cutoff tracks accurately at the standard 48kHz production sample rate — pitch-tracking self-oscillation is reliable at the most common DAW sample rate.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=200 sr=96000
entries.what: Same M12 self-osc pitch gate at fc=200 Hz/96000 Hz sample rate (a high-quality render/host rate).
entries.why: 96 kHz is the coarse-characterization-grid's fixed host rate (per CharacterizationRunnerTests' coarseGrid) — this point cross-validates self-osc tuning at that same rate.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation pitch is mistuned at 96 kHz specifically — since this is also the characterization harness's standard host rate, compare against any characterization-runner self-osc measurements at the same rate.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 200Hz, 96kHz sample rate, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-0.75% via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a low cutoff tracks accurately even at a high 96kHz sample rate — the pitch-tracking accuracy doesn't depend on running at a specific sample rate.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=1000 sr=44100
entries.what: Same M12 self-osc pitch gate at fc=1000 Hz/44100 Hz — a mid-range musical pitch at the classic CD sample rate.
entries.why: 1000 Hz is a common reference/test-tone pitch used throughout the harness for other measurements (e.g. most filter-characterization tests default to fc=1000) — this cross-validates self-osc tuning at the harness's most-used reference frequency.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation pitch is mistuned at the harness's standard 1000 Hz reference point at 44.1kHz — a regression at the most heavily-tested frequency in the whole suite.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 1000Hz, 44.1kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-0.75% via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a mid-range cutoff (the most musically common register) tracks accurately at 44.1kHz — pitched filter self-oscillation is reliable in its most-used range.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=1000 sr=48000
entries.what: Same M12 self-osc pitch gate at fc=1000 Hz/48000 Hz.
entries.why: Combines the harness's two most common reference values (1000 Hz cutoff, 48 kHz rate) into one grid point.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation pitch is mistuned at the harness's most standard reference point (1kHz/48kHz) — likely the first place a real tuning regression would show up.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 1000Hz, 48kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-0.75% via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a mid-range cutoff tracks accurately at the standard 48kHz production rate — the mid-range/most-common-rate combination, the everyday use case, is verified in tune.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=1000 sr=96000
entries.what: Same M12 self-osc pitch gate at fc=1000 Hz/96000 Hz.
entries.why: Completes the fc=1000 row of the M12 3x3 grid across all three tested sample rates.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation pitch is mistuned at 1kHz/96kHz — compare against the 1kHz/44.1kHz and 1kHz/48kHz results to see if the error scales with sample rate.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 1000Hz, 96kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-0.75% via testdsp::Gate — the filter's own cutoff setting is the reference, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a mid-range cutoff tracks accurately even at 96kHz — confirms the mid-range accuracy holds across sample rates too.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=5000 sr=44100
entries.what: Same M12 setup at fc=5000 Hz/44100 Hz, but gated at 3.0% (not 0.75%) since fc>4kHz — the code comment documents this as the WORST-CASE point in the grid (~-0.60% measured), an inherent gray-box delayed-feedback pitch shift re-anchored after the Q27 bounded-resonance fix.
entries.why: 5000 Hz exceeds the ±3%-strict/±0.75%-gated boundary the filter-validation standard draws at ~4kHz — this is a documented, KNOWN, non-gating-tight artifact of the gray-box (rail-set limit cycle) resonance model, not a hidden bug, and the fix's rail-setting changed the exact detuning amount.
entries.deviationMeans: High-frequency self-oscillation tuning has moved beyond its already-loose 3% tolerance — since this point is explicitly called out as the standard's worst case and a known Q27-adjacent gray-box artifact, treat a regression here as either the artifact worsening (still within design) or a genuine new bug depending on how far it moved.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 5000Hz, 44.1kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at the wider +-3% band the ratified standard applies above 4kHz via testdsp::Gate — documented as the worst-observed case (~1.5% measured error, a known gray-box delayed-feedback pitch-shift artifact, candidate for future SP-D hardware calibration).
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a high cutoff (5kHz) still tracks the Cutoff knob within the wider, explicitly-relaxed tolerance the ratified standard allows for this known high-frequency artifact — the detuning at high register is measured, bounded, and documented as an accepted characteristic rather than an unbounded, unnoticed drift.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=5000 sr=48000
entries.what: Same M12 setup at fc=5000 Hz/48000 Hz, gated at 3.0%; code comment cites ~2.9% measured error at this exact point post-Q27-fix.
entries.why: This is the single tightest-margin point in the entire M12 grid (measured ~2.9% against a 3.0% gate) — the code comment explicitly flags fc=5000 at 44.1k/48k as the standard's worst case.
entries.deviationMeans: This is the M12 grid point closest to its own gate threshold — even a small further detuning regression here would flip it from pass to fail before any other grid point does, making it an early-warning canary for gray-box tuning drift.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 5000Hz, 48kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-3% (documented worst case ~2.9% measured error at this exact combination) via testdsp::Gate.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a high cutoff and the standard 48kHz rate — the single worst-measured combination in the whole M12 sweep — still stays within the ratified, explicitly-relaxed tolerance; the known high-frequency detuning artifact is bounded, not unbounded.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M12 self-osc pitch (std gate) @ fc=5000 sr=96000
entries.what: Same M12 setup at fc=5000 Hz/96000 Hz, gated at 3.0%.
entries.why: Completes the fc=5000 row of the M12 grid; at 96 kHz the same gray-box delayed-feedback artifact is expected to be smaller (proportionally more headroom between fc and Nyquist) than at 44.1k/48k.
entries.deviationMeans: High-frequency self-osc tuning at the highest tested sample rate has regressed — if 44.1k/48k stay within tolerance but this fails, it would suggest a rate-dependent scaling bug rather than the general gray-box artifact.
entries.compares: Zero-crossing-derived pitch of BP-mode self-oscillation at cutoff 5000Hz, 96kHz, against the literal cutoff frequency, gated at +-3% via testdsp::Gate.
entries.succeedingMeans: Self-oscillation at a high cutoff tracks well within tolerance at a high sample rate (96kHz reduces the delayed-feedback pitch-shift artifact seen at lower rates) — confirms the artifact is rate-dependent and genuinely improves at higher oversampling-adjacent rates.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M13 low-level linear (amp=0.001, f=200 Hz, fc=1000)
entries.what: At tiny amplitude (0.001) with resSat=0, gates measured LP magnitude at 200 Hz against the analytic LP formula within 0.1 dB (a tighter tolerance than M10's 0.5 dB, since low-level signal should be essentially perfectly linear).
entries.why: M13 — proves the cell behaves as a true linear filter at whisper-quiet input levels, the small-signal reference point every large-signal/level-response measurement (LevelResponseTests, HuggettBoundedResonanceTests' gain-vs-level test) implicitly assumes as its 'linear regime' baseline.
entries.deviationMeans: Even at tiny input levels, the cell isn't matching its linear analytic prediction as tightly as expected — since level-response measurements elsewhere assume small-signal behavior IS the linear baseline, this would undermine the reference point those tests compare against.
entries.compares: Measured LP magnitude (dB) at a tiny amplitude (0.001, deep small-signal regime, res-sat off) against the closed-form analytic LP formula, gated at a tight 0.1dB via testdsp::Gate — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: At very quiet playing levels, the nonlinear filter cell behaves as a genuinely linear filter matching textbook theory almost exactly — quiet passages aren't secretly colored by residual nonlinearity even when the saturator is nominally off.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M15 denormal flush (tail â¤ -300 dB after 1 s silence)
entries.what: Kicks an NlSvfCell at res=0.3 (just below self-osc) with an impulse, runs 1s of silence, and gates the tail-half (0.5-1.0s) energy at <= -300 dB.
entries.why: M15 - proves the filter's decaying resonance tail eventually flushes to true digital silence (denormal-safe) rather than hanging at a tiny non-zero level indefinitely, which would waste CPU on denormal-slow floating point or leave an inaudible-but-technically-nonzero DC-adjacent tail.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter's decay tail is not reaching true silence within 1 second - could indicate a denormal-handling regression (CPU-expensive denormal floats) or a subtle state leak that prevents full decay.
entries.compares: Tail energy (dB, last 0.5s of 1s post-kick silence) at a near-self-oscillation-but-decaying resonance (0.3) against a fixed ceiling of -300 dB via testdsp::Gate — a denormal-number-handling/decay-to-silence contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: A ringing filter genuinely decays all the way to silence (not stuck at a denormal-number CPU-load floor) — a sustained note's filter ring-out doesn't silently burn CPU on denormal floating-point numbers after the audible tail has ended.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M9 zipper NSR <= static baseline + 3 dB
entries.what: Holds a bin-aligned 200 Hz tone while ramping cutoff 400->4000 Hz over 128-sample blocks (2s), measures inharmonic energy (zipper noise) via NSR, and gates it at <= -12.0 dB — an explicitly-documented REGRESSION anchor (measured -15.06 dB baseline + 3 dB headroom), not a 'zipper-free' claim, since the cell has no cutoff smoothing yet.
entries.why: M9 — the code comment is explicit that this catches a WORSENING of a KNOWN, accepted zipper artifact (the v5 per-parameter smoothing sub-step is unbuilt), pending either cutoff smoothing landing (which would let the gate tighten toward the static baseline) or a calibration decision about whether -15 dB matches real synths.
entries.deviationMeans: Per-block cutoff automation is producing MORE zipper noise than the already-accepted baseline — since -15 dB zipper is a known, accepted-for-now limitation (not itself a bug), a regression here means it got WORSE, not that zipper exists at all.
entries.compares: Inharmonic energy (NSR dB) of a held tone while the cutoff is ramped once-per-128-sample-block from 400Hz to 4000Hz over 2 seconds, against a fixed regression anchor of -12.0dB (measured baseline -15.06dB + 3dB headroom, committed 2026-06-20) via testdsp::Gate — explicitly documented as NOT a 'zipper-free' claim (the cell has no cutoff smoothing yet — a known, tracked, unbuilt v5 sub-step), only a guard against the zipper artifact getting WORSE than its current measured level.
entries.succeedingMeans: Sweeping the cutoff parameter in real time (e.g. a filter-cutoff LFO or envelope) doesn't produce MORE audible per-block zipper noise than the already-known, already-accepted baseline amount — this test is honest that today's zipper noise (~-15dB, audibly present) is a known limitation awaiting the unbuilt cutoff-smoothing feature, not a claim that cutoff sweeps sound clean today.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14a DC removed (tail |mean| <= 0.02)
entries.what: Feeds 0.5 DC + 200 Hz sine through DcBlocker for 8192 samples and gates the tail (samples 6000-8192) mean absolute value at <= 0.02 via testdsp::Gate.
entries.why: M14a in the acceptance-criterion metric numbering — a gated, machine-readable fixture-harness port of the same DC-removal property HuggettNonlinearTests checks with a plain expect(), now wired through the Gate/printf reporting convention used by the deeper characterization fixtures.
entries.deviationMeans: DC removal has regressed against the formal M14a gate threshold — since this is a port/duplicate of HuggettNonlinearTests' DcBlocker check, compare both to see if the regression is DcBlocker itself or specific to this harness's gate wiring.
entries.compares: Mean of the settled tail (post-warm-up) of a DC-blocked (0.5 DC + 200Hz sine) signal against a fixed ceiling of 0.02 via testdsp::Gate — an analytic DC-removal contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The production DC-blocking stage genuinely removes a constant offset injected upstream — this is the harness's committed regression gate mirroring the equivalent unit test, protecting against silent DC buildup that could eat headroom or trip downstream limiters.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14b audio RMS preserved (>= 0.5x input)
entries.what: On the same 0.5-DC+200Hz-sine signal, compares tail-window RMS of output vs input and gates output RMS at >= 0.5x input RMS.
entries.why: M14b — proves DC removal isn't achieved by also attenuating the legitimate AC audio content riding on the DC offset (a naive/aggressive DC blocker could remove both).
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker is over-attenuating audio content along with the DC offset — every asymmetric-saturator-driven signal passing through a DcBlocker downstream would lose audible level, not just DC.
entries.compares: Tail RMS of the DC-blocked output against half the tail RMS of the original (pre-blocking) input signal, requiring output RMS >= 0.5x input via testdsp::Gate — a relative self-comparison against the signal's own input level, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Removing DC offset doesn't collaterally damage the actual audio content — a harness-level regression gate confirming the DC blocker stays a surgical DC-removal tool, not a heavy-handed filter that also eats signal.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14c L/R independence (opposite DC, both -> 0)
entries.what: Feeds +0.5 DC to channel 0 and -0.5 DC to channel 1 of the same DcBlocker instance; gates both channels' final sample at <= 0.05 absolute value (both converge toward zero independently).
entries.why: M14c, an explicit port of HuggettNonlinearTests' 'DcBlocker keeps L/R state independent' check (per the file's header comment) into the gated fixture-harness convention — a state-sharing bug would let opposite-sign DC cross-contaminate and at least one channel would fail to converge.
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker's L/R state has become coupled — identical regression concern to the original HuggettNonlinearTests version; check both to see if the bug is in DcBlocker itself or specific to this harness wiring.
entries.compares: Final processed samples on both channels, after feeding opposite-sign DC offsets (+0.5 L, -0.5 R) for 8192 samples, each against a fixed convergence threshold of 0.05 via testdsp::Gate — proves per-channel convergence without cross-contamination, no golden file; a harness-level port of the equivalent unit test.
entries.succeedingMeans: The stereo DC blocker's L/R state independence is a standing, gated regression check — an asymmetric DC condition on one channel can never leak into or distort the other channel's DC removal, committed as part of the ongoing regression battery, not just a one-off unit test.

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M10 HP corner: highs pass, lows cut (12 vs 24)
entries.what: Gates HuggettHpStage at fc=1000: highs (8kHz, db12) pass >= 0.7, lows (200Hz, db12) cut <= 0.1, 24dB is steeper than 12dB below the corner, and 24dB strongly cuts lows (<=0.04) — a gated port of HpPreFilterTests' plain-expect version of the same properties.
entries.why: M10 in the acceptance-criterion numbering — the gated-harness counterpart to HpPreFilterTests, using the same measurement helper pattern (peakMag) explicitly noted in the code comment as matching HpPreFilterTests' 'mag' lambda exactly.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's basic corner/slope behavior has regressed against the formal M10 gate — cross-check against HpPreFilterTests (the original, non-gated version) to see if both fail together (a real DSP regression) or just this harness (a gate-wiring issue).
entries.compares: Peak magnitude at 8kHz (passband, >=0.7), 200Hz at 12dB slope (<=0.1), 200Hz at 24dB slope (<=0.04, and explicitly less than the 12dB value) — the same fixed analytic HP contract as HpPreFilterTests, re-gated as a standing harness check via testdsp::Gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The HP pre-filter's basic pass-highs/cut-lows shape and its 24dB-steeper-than-12dB slope behavior are committed regression gates in the ongoing battery — this specific filter-shape contract can't silently regress without the harness catching it.

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M11 resonant peak above HP corner
entries.what: At res=0.5 (small-signal, linear regime), scans 25 points from 900-1400 Hz around a 1000 Hz HP corner, finds the peak magnitude, and gates its frequency within 2% of fc and its height at least 1 dB above a passband reference (measured at 8000 Hz) — a resonant bump analytically expected from the 2-pole HP transfer function H_hp = u^2/sqrt((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2).
entries.why: M11 — this is a HP-stage-specific resonant-peak test with no direct HpPreFilterTests counterpart (a genuinely new fixture-harness measurement), verifying the HP stage's resonance behaves per the documented analytic HP peak formula, not just that it self-oscillates safely at max resonance.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's resonant peak (at moderate, non-extreme resonance) is missing, mistuned, or too subtle — the HP stage's Q/resonance behavior in its normal playing range (not just the max-resonance self-osc edge case) has regressed.
entries.compares: A frequency-scanned (25-point, 900-1400Hz) peak search of the HP stage's magnitude response at resonance 0.5, requiring the peak frequency land within +-2% of the configured cutoff (1000Hz) and the peak height exceed the passband reference level by >=1dB — an analytic 2-pole HP-resonance-peak contract (documented closed form H_hp = u^2/sqrt((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2)), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The HP stage's resonance control produces a genuine resonant emphasis just above the cutoff frequency, correctly located and with real measurable height — not just a generic slope steepening, the HP resonance knob creates the classic filter-peak character a HP resonance control is expected to have.

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M2 self-osc bounded <= 4.0 (max res, db24)
entries.what: Kicks HuggettHpStage at fc=1200/res=1.0/24dB with an impulse, runs 24000 samples, and gates the settled peak at <= 4.0 with no NaN/Inf — a gated port of HpPreFilterTests' self-oscillation-bounded check.
entries.why: M2 in the acceptance-criterion numbering — the same Q27-adjacent bounded-resonance safety property (state-blow-up prevention) as HpPreFilterTests, now wired through the formal Gate/M-number convention used by the deeper fixture harnesses.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's max-resonance self-oscillation is unbounded against the formal M2 gate — cross-check against HpPreFilterTests to see if the underlying DSP regressed (both fail) or only this harness's gate wiring did.
entries.compares: Peak post-settling output amplitude of a kicked HP stage at maximum resonance (1.0, db24) against a fixed ceiling of 4.0, plus std::isfinite on every sample — a stability/safety bound at the filter's most extreme setting, no golden file; a harness-level port of the equivalent unit test.
entries.succeedingMeans: Maxing out the HP pre-filter's resonance stays safely bounded as a standing, gated regression check — this specific stability guarantee is part of the ongoing battery, not just a one-off assertion that could bitrot unnoticed.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Moog FUT: supports LP/BP/HP, rejects Notch
entries.what: Asserts the Moog FUT reports supports(LP24)==true, supports(BP)==true, supports(Notch)==false, and name()=="moog".
entries.why: Mode-support declarations gate which measurement batteries CharacterizationRunner attempts against a model — Moog genuinely has no Notch mode (unlike Huggett's nine-routing set, Q14), so the harness must not try to measure a mode the model can't produce.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's declared mode support no longer matches its real capabilities — either the runner will try to measure a nonexistent Notch response (garbage data) or skip a mode Moog actually now supports.
entries.compares: The Moog FilterUnderTest wrapper's supports() for LP24/BP/Notch against literal expected booleans (true/true/false), and its name() against the literal string "moog" — fixed structural/capability contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The device-characterization harness's Moog adapter correctly reports which filter modes the real Moog model actually has (no Notch, since the authentic Moog ladder never had one) — the characterization battery can't accidentally run or expect results from a mode Moog doesn't support.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Huggett FUT: supports Notch
entries.what: Asserts the Huggett FUT reports supports(Notch)==true and name()=="huggett".
entries.why: Huggett's Notch mode is part of the nine-routing dual-filter set shipped in Q14/v5.2; this is the measurement-harness-side declaration that lets CharacterizationRunnerTests' Notch battery run for Huggett specifically.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett's Notch mode is no longer declared as supported — the characterization runner would skip measuring it, silently dropping coverage of one of the nine shipped routings.
entries.compares: The Huggett FilterUnderTest wrapper's supports(Notch) against the literal expectation true, and its name() against the literal string "huggett" — fixed structural contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The device-characterization harness correctly recognizes that Huggett (unlike Moog) does support Notch mode — the characterization battery can exercise Huggett's Notch routing, a capability Moog genuinely lacks.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Moog LP24 passes bass, rejects treble (socket drives a real measurement)
entries.what: Sets Moog to LP24 fc=1000 Hz via the OperatingPoint socket and measures 100 Hz and 8000 Hz; asserts 100 Hz reads above -3 dB (near passband) and 8000 Hz reads below -24 dB (strongly attenuated).
entries.why: End-to-end proof that the FilterUnderTest's OperatingPoint parameterization socket actually drives a real, correctly-shaped Moog measurement — not just that the interface compiles, but that setting an operating point produces the expected filter behavior.
entries.deviationMeans: Setting an OperatingPoint on the Moog FUT no longer produces the expected LP shape — either the socket isn't correctly forwarding to the real MoogLadder, or the ladder itself has a basic LP regression.
entries.compares: Stepped-sine-measured magnitude (dB) at 100Hz against a fixed floor (>-3.0dB, near-passband) and at 8000Hz against a fixed ceiling (<-24.0dB, strongly attenuated), driven through the abstract FilterUnderTest socket rather than the concrete MoogLadder class directly — an analytic passband/stopband contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The device-characterization framework's generic measurement socket (FilterUnderTest, the abstraction that lets any filter be measured the same way) actually drives a real, correct measurement of Moog — the socket isn't a hollow interface, it genuinely reaches and measures the real DSP.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / OS factors are in-band transparent (LP, probe well below cutoff)
entries.what: Measures Moog LP24 fc=2000 Hz at 200 Hz across osFactor {1,2,4,8} and asserts each oversampled measurement stays within 0.5 dB of the os1 baseline.
entries.why: In-band content well below cutoff should be essentially unaffected by the OS factor chosen (Q12's OS policy) — this is the harness-level guard that oversampling changes cost/aliasing behavior, not the passband gain of unrelated in-band content.
entries.deviationMeans: Changing the oversampling factor is altering in-band gain at a frequency nowhere near the filter's transition — the OS-path implementation (Halfband2x, Q23) has a gain/level bug independent of its aliasing performance.
entries.compares: Stepped-sine-measured magnitude (dB) at a fixed in-band probe (200Hz, cutoff 2000Hz) at oversampling factors 2/4/8 against the same probe's magnitude at factor 1 (base rate), each within 0.5dB — a relative self-comparison across the device's own OS setting, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Changing the oversampling factor doesn't alter a filter's measured in-band response — the characterization framework's OS-factor plumbing is transparent for content well within the passband, so OS-factor comparisons elsewhere in the battery aren't confounded by an in-band level shift from OS itself.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / coarseGrid is bounded: 96k only, OS {1,2,4,8}, live only
entries.what: Asserts chz::coarseGrid() is fixed to a single 96 kHz host rate, all four OS factors {1,2,4,8}, and Live-only osMode (no Render mode).
entries.why: coarseGrid() is the 10-25 minute full characterization sweep; pinning its shape prevents an accidental grid explosion (e.g. adding host rates or Render mode) from silently multiplying runtime beyond the documented budget — directly relevant to the Q28 grid-economy problem (36k-point fullGrid pricing).
entries.deviationMeans: The coarse characterization grid's dimensions have changed — check the actual runtime against the documented 10-25 min budget; an expansion here compounds with Q28's already-expensive fullGrid.
entries.compares: chz::coarseGrid()'s hostRates/osFactors/osModes against literal fixed expectations (single 96000Hz rate, all four OS factors {1,2,4,8}, Live mode only) — a fixed configuration-shape contract, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The full, expensive 'coarse grid' characterization sweep (10-25 minutes, mentioned as too slow for the fast per-commit gate) is scoped exactly as documented — anyone running the deep characterization tool knows precisely what parameter space it covers before committing to the long runtime.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / runner produces LP24 headline metrics for Moog and writes a CSV
entries.what: Runs a tiny 1-point Moog LP24 fc=1000 grid and asserts corner_hz lands in 200-2000 Hz (the authentic 4-pole cascade corner sits at ~0.44*fc, not fc, per the code comment) and slope_db_oct < -3.0, plus that response.csv is written to the output dir.
entries.why: Documents and pins the hardware-accurate detail that a 4-pole Moog cascade's -3 dB point is NOT at the nominal cutoff but at sqrt(2^0.25-1)*fc ≈ 0.435*fc — a naive +-1-octave sanity window would wrongly flag correct behavior as a bug, hence the widened +2/-2.3 octave window.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the corner has moved outside the accepted hardware-accurate range (real regression) or the window itself needs adjusting — check against the FilterUnderTestTests stopband-attenuation test before concluding either way.
entries.compares: The runner's summary corner_hz for Moog LP24 fc=1000 against a fixed sanity range (200-2000Hz, deliberately widened from a naive +-1-octave window to +2/-2.3 octaves to accommodate the AUTHENTIC 4-pole-cascade corner landing at ~0.44*fc~=440Hz rather than exactly fc), and slope_db_oct against a fixed ceiling (<-3.0, confirms rolloff, not the asymptotic -24dB/oct which only appears well into the stopband) — analytic/documented hardware-accurate-behavior bounds, no golden file; also checks response.csv was written to disk.
entries.succeedingMeans: The characterization runner correctly measures and reports Moog's real, hardware-accurate -3dB corner location (which is intentionally NOT at the naive cutoff frequency, because a genuine 4-pole ladder cascade's combined corner sits lower than any single stage's corner) — the runner's headline numbers reflect authentic filter physics, not a naive expectation that would actually be wrong for a real ladder filter.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / B2+B3: resonance.csv + distortion.csv exist; B2/B3 summary keys present and finite
entries.what: Runs Moog LP24 at res={0,0.9} and osFactors={1,2}; asserts resonance.csv and distortion.csv are written and that selfosc_cents_err, thd_db, alias_db@os1, alias_db@os2 summary keys are each either a plausible finite value (> -900) or exactly the -1.0 not-detected sentinel.
entries.why: Fixes a documented bug ("FIX 5") where isfinite(-1.0) is trivially true, making the old finite-OR-sentinel check dead code that would have let an absurd value like -5756 cents pass silently; this test pins the corrected plausible-range-or-exact-sentinel logic.
entries.deviationMeans: A B2 (self-osc pitch) or B3 (THD/aliasing) battery metric is producing an implausible value that isn't the sentinel — the runner's self-osc detection or THD/alias measurement has regressed.
entries.compares: Presence of resonance.csv and distortion.csv output files, plus presence and plausibility of 5 summary keys (selfosc_cents_err, thd_db, alias_db@os1, alias_db@os2) each checked against either a plausible finite range (>-900) or the documented exact sentinel value -1.0 (meaning 'not detected') — a structural/plausibility contract explicitly designed to catch a specific prior bug (FIX 5: isfinite(-1.0) being true made the old check accept any bogus value like -5756), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The B2 (self-oscillation) and B3 (distortion/aliasing) characterization batteries produce their output files and summary keys with genuinely plausible values (or an honest 'not detected' sentinel) rather than silently accepting nonsense numbers — this closes a real historical bug where a garbage measurement could have passed undetected.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / Huggett runs the same battery (model-agnostic socket) incl. Notch
entries.what: Runs the Huggett FUT through LP24 and Notch modes and asserts both produce a corner_hz summary key and response.csv is written; values are logged, not tightly bounded (this test is about model-agnosticism, not Huggett voicing).
entries.why: Proves the CharacterizationRunner socket (FilterUnderTest interface) is genuinely model-agnostic — the same runner code drives both Moog and Huggett, including Huggett's extra Notch mode (part of the nine-routing set from Q14), without per-model special-casing.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner no longer produces results for Huggett or its Notch mode — the FilterUnderTest abstraction has broken for a specific model or mode, undermining the 'one harness for every filter model' design.
entries.compares: Presence of corner_hz summary keys for both huggett/LP24 and huggett/Notch, plus response.csv written to disk — a structural/completeness check proving the SAME runner code ran a mode (Notch) that only Huggett supports, no golden file (values are logged, not gated, per the file's stated model-agnosticism focus).
entries.succeedingMeans: The exact same characterization runner code, unmodified, correctly runs the full battery against a completely different filter model (Huggett instead of Moog) including a mode (Notch) the other model doesn't even have — the framework is genuinely model-agnostic, not secretly Moog-shaped code that happens to also compile against Huggett.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / aliasing decreases as OS factor rises (Moog, driven LP)
entries.what: Runs a driven (drive=1.0) Moog LP24 fc=4000 across osFactors {1,2,4,8} and asserts alias_db at os8 is more than 3 dB lower than at os1.
entries.why: The whole point of oversampling the drive stages (Q12's ADAA/OS policy) is to reduce audible aliasing from the nonlinearity; this is the end-to-end proof that raising OS factor actually buys a measurable aliasing reduction, not just added CPU cost.
entries.deviationMeans: Higher oversampling factors are no longer reducing aliasing on a driven filter — the OS/anti-aliasing pipeline (Q12, or the Q23 halfband optimization) may have a correctness regression that makes the extra CPU cost pointless.
entries.compares: Measured alias_db at oversampling factor 8 against alias_db at factor 1 (same hot, driven LP operating point: fc=4000Hz, drive=1.0), requiring the os8 value be more than 3dB lower (less aliasing) — a relative self-comparison across the device's own OS setting, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: For a real, driven filter under a demanding operating point, raising the oversampling factor genuinely and measurably reduces aliasing as reported by the characterization framework's own aliasing metric — the framework's OS-vs-aliasing relationship reporting matches the physics it's supposed to measure.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / Moog HP fc4000: methods agree in-band (deep stopband is noise-limited)
entries.what: Measures Moog HP fc=4000 via both stepped-sine and ESS across 200 probes 20 Hz-24 kHz; asserts the IN-BAND (within 40 dB of passband peak) method delta is < 1.0 dB, while documenting that the UNSCOPED delta (including the ~-60 dB+ deep stopband where both methods hit their noise floor and scatter ~5 dB) would be a meaningless ~90 dB.
entries.why: Regression test for a method-agreement SCOPING bug — an HP filter's deep stopband is where measurement noise dominates the signal, so comparing methods there measures noise-floor scatter, not filter (dis)agreement; this pins the in-band-only comparison as the meaningful metric.
entries.deviationMeans: The in-band method-agreement window itself has broken, or the passband-relative windowing logic regressed — if this starts failing, check whether the 40 dB in-band definition still correctly excludes the noise-limited stopband.
entries.compares: In-band (within 40dB of the passband peak) max magnitude-dB delta between stepped-sine and ESS measurement methods for a real Moog HP filter, against a fixed ceiling of <1.0dB, explicitly SCOPED away from the deep stopband (which reads ~-60dB and below, where both methods hit their noise floor and scatter ~5dB, making the unscoped max delta ~90dB and meaningless) — this is the regression fix for a specific method-agreement scoping bug, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The dual-method cross-check correctly recognizes that deep-stopband disagreement between two measurement techniques is noise-floor scatter, not a real filter discrepancy, and still confirms genuine agreement where it matters (the passband and transition band) — the method-agreement gate is scoped intelligently enough not to produce false alarms from measuring silence.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: LP24 fc1000 slope_db_oct <= -3.0 (rolling off)
entries.what: Runs a fast always-on gate grid (LP24, fc=1000 Hz, res 0/0.9, os1, hostSR 96k, 40 probes) on the Moog FUT and asserts slope_db_oct <= -3.0 dB/oct, confirming the filter genuinely rolls off above its corner.
entries.why: This is the CI-fast (~5s added) spec gate distinguishing a working LP filter from a broken/flat one, run on every build — the coarse full characterization grid (10-25 min) is too slow to gate on.
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog LP24 filter is no longer rolling off above its cutoff at a basic sanity threshold — a fundamental filter-shape regression, not a subtle voicing change.
entries.compares: Moog's measured slope_db_oct (from the small always-on CI gate grid, not the 10-25min coarseGrid) against a fixed ceiling of -3.0dB/oct via testdsp::Gate — a documented sanity threshold (measured Moog is actually ~-7dB/oct; the gate just confirms genuine rolloff, not the specific slope value), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Every CI run confirms Moog's LP24 filter is still genuinely rolling off above its corner frequency — a fast, always-on tripwire that would catch a filter that stopped filtering at all, running in ~5 extra seconds added to the full suite.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: LP24 fc1000 method_delta_db <= 1.0 (in-band dual-method agreement)
entries.what: Asserts the in-band (within 40 dB of passband peak) max magnitude delta between the stepped-sine and ESS measurement methods stays <= 1.0 dB for Moog LP24 fc=1000 in the gate grid (measured ~0.31 dB).
entries.why: The dual-method agreement gate (filter-validation harness's core trust mechanism) proves the measured response isn't a measurement-method artifact; if the two independent methods disagree, neither can be trusted as ground truth.
entries.deviationMeans: Stepped-sine and ESS measurements of the same filter now disagree beyond gate tolerance — either the filter's response has become method-sensitive (e.g. time-varying/nonlinear in a way that breaks LTI assumptions) or one of the measurement methods has a bug.
entries.compares: Moog's measured in-band method_delta_db (stepped-sine vs ESS agreement) against a fixed ceiling of 1.0dB via testdsp::Gate — a documented sanity threshold (measured is actually ~0.31dB, well under the gate), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Every CI run confirms the two independent measurement methods still agree closely on Moog's real response — an always-on tripwire for measurement-tooling regressions, not just DSP regressions.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: self-golden (LP24/fc1000 headline metrics)
entries.what: Compares Moog's corner_hz, slope_db_oct, method_delta_db, selfosc_cents_err, peak_gain_db, passband_gain_db, noise_floor_dbfs and noise_floor_dbfsA against committed golden baselines (tests/golden/moog/baseline.csv) with per-metric tolerances.
entries.why: Self-golden regression catches ANY drift in Moog's headline characterization numbers between commits, even changes too subtle for the coarse spec gates above to catch — golden updates require BERNIE_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 and a justified commit message per the build rails.
entries.deviationMeans: One of Moog's headline metrics moved outside its golden tolerance — investigate whether this is an intentional voicing/DSP change (requires a golden regen + commit justification) or an unintended regression.
entries.compares: Moog's corner_hz/slope_db_oct/method_delta_db/selfosc_cents_err/peak_gain_db/passband_gain_db/noise_floor_dbfs/noise_floor_dbfsA against the committed golden file tests/golden/moog/baseline.csv (regenerable via BERNIE_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1), each within its own documented tolerance (e.g. corner_hz within 50Hz, slope within 2dB/oct) — an external golden CSV file, the exact worked-example pattern.
entries.succeedingMeans: Moog's complete headline characterization fingerprint (corner location, slope, method agreement, self-oscillation pitch accuracy, gain levels, noise floor) is pinned to a specific approved baseline — any silent DSP change to Moog that shifts these numbers is caught immediately in CI, not discovered later by ear.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: LP24 fc1000 slope_db_oct <= -3.0 (rolling off)
entries.what: Same gate-grid slope sanity check as the Moog case but for Huggett LP24 fc=1000 — asserts slope_db_oct <= -3.0 dB/oct.
entries.why: Mirrors the Moog gate for the other live filter model; both models must pass the same fast CI sanity check since either can be selected live via spine.filterModel (ADR-0011).
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett LP24 is no longer rolling off at a basic sanity threshold — check for a regression in the dual-SVF-cell topology (Q13) or the Q27 bounded-resonance fix's interaction with the base LP response.
entries.compares: Huggett's measured slope_db_oct (small always-on CI gate grid) against a fixed ceiling of -3.0dB/oct via testdsp::Gate — the same documented sanity threshold as Moog's equivalent gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Every CI run confirms Huggett's LP24 filter is still genuinely rolling off above its corner — the same fast always-on tripwire as Moog's, applied to the second filter model.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: LP24 fc1000 method_delta_db <= 1.0 (in-band dual-method agreement)
entries.what: Same in-band stepped-sine-vs-ESS agreement check as Moog's, applied to Huggett LP24 fc=1000, asserting <= 1.0 dB delta.
entries.why: Ensures the dual-method trust guarantee holds for Huggett too, including after the Q27 resonance-loop fix which changed its nonlinear behavior at high resonance.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett's two measurement methods disagree beyond tolerance — could indicate the Q27 fix (or any subsequent nonlinear change) introduced time-variance or instability that breaks the LTI measurement assumption.
entries.compares: Huggett's measured in-band method_delta_db against a fixed ceiling of 1.0dB via testdsp::Gate — the same documented sanity threshold as Moog's equivalent gate, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Every CI run confirms the two independent measurement methods still agree closely on Huggett's real response — the measurement-tooling tripwire applies equally to both production filter models.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: self-golden (LP24/fc1000 headline metrics)
entries.what: Compares Huggett's headline metrics (corner_hz, slope_db_oct, method_delta_db, peak_gain_db, passband_gain_db, noise floors — self-osc golden skipped, includeSelfOsc=false) against tests/golden/huggett/baseline.csv.
entries.why: Huggett's self-osc golden is deliberately excluded here (unlike Moog) because Huggett doesn't reliably self-oscillate at this gate grid's resonance; the peak_gain_db check is explicitly noted as a coarse-grid regression PROXY, not the true peak (true peak/disparity is LevelDisparityTests' job).
entries.deviationMeans: A Huggett headline metric drifted from its golden baseline — given the Q27 history (resonance loop defect/fix) treat any peak_gain_db or slope drift with extra scrutiny before accepting a golden regen.
entries.compares: Huggett's corner_hz/slope_db_oct/method_delta_db/peak_gain_db/passband_gain_db/noise_floor_dbfs/noise_floor_dbfsA (no selfosc_cents_err — Huggett doesn't reliably self-oscillate at this grid point) against the committed golden file tests/golden/huggett/baseline.csv, each within documented tolerance — an external golden CSV file. NOTE: peak_gain_db here (~25.3dB) is the coarse 40-probe gate-grid value, an explicitly-documented STABLE PROXY, not Huggett's true dense-sweep peak (measured separately, far higher, in LevelDisparityTests).
entries.succeedingMeans: Huggett's complete headline characterization fingerprint is pinned to a specific approved baseline in the same way Moog's is — any silent DSP change to Huggett is caught in CI; this test's peak_gain_db number should NOT be read as Huggett's true resonant peak level (that requires the dense sweep elsewhere), only as a fast regression tripwire on this specific coarse measurement.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / descriptor reports kind / excitation / name
entries.what: Verifies a minimal PassthroughDevice implementing DeviceUnderTest reports kind()==TransferFunction, excitation()==InputSweep, and name()=="passthrough" through the abstract base.
entries.why: DeviceUnderTest is the harness-wide polymorphic contract every measured thing (real filters, reference devices, calibration tones) implements; this is the minimal-implementation proof that the contract's descriptor methods work with nothing else present.
entries.deviationMeans: The DeviceUnderTest base interface's descriptor methods are broken for even a trivial implementation — every concrete device built on this abstraction is suspect.
entries.compares: A minimal PassthroughDevice's kind()/excitation()/name() against the literal expected values (TransferFunction, InputSweep, "passthrough") accessed only through the abstract DeviceUnderTest base class — a fixed interface-contract check, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The device-characterization framework's most basic abstraction (any measurable device reports its own kind/excitation/name) works correctly through virtual dispatch on a trivial test device — the foundational contract every real filter measurement builds on is sound.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / L0 ruler measures a device through the base (unity ~0 dB)
entries.what: Measures the unity-gain PassthroughDevice via testdsp::SteppedSine::transfer called through the DeviceUnderTest& abstract reference at 100/1000/10000 Hz; asserts every magnitude reads within 0.01 dB of 0 (true unity).
entries.why: Proves the L0 (lowest-level) stepped-sine ruler engine works when driven through virtual dispatch on the abstract base, not just via a concrete type — this is the polymorphism guarantee the whole runner architecture depends on.
entries.deviationMeans: Measuring through the abstract DeviceUnderTest& reference no longer produces correct results even for the simplest possible device — a fundamental break in the measurement-engine/interface boundary.
entries.compares: Stepped-sine-measured magnitude (dB) of a unity-gain passthrough device, measured only through the abstract DeviceUnderTest& base reference, against the literal expectation ~0 dB (within 0.01) at 3 frequencies — an analytic identity (a true passthrough must read exactly 0 dB), no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The characterization framework's base measurement engine (the 'L0 ruler') correctly measures a known-trivial device through pure virtual dispatch — the measurement machinery itself introduces no error before it's ever pointed at a real, more complex filter.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / real filter factories report TransferFunction / InputSweep
entries.what: Upcasts chz::makeMoogFut() and makeHuggettFut() to DeviceUnderTest& and asserts both report kind()==TransferFunction, excitation()==InputSweep, and correct names ("moog"/"huggett").
entries.why: Proves FilterUnderTest IS-A DeviceUnderTest for both real filter models — the upcast is the load-bearing relationship that lets CharacterizationRunner treat filters and reference/calibration devices uniformly.
entries.deviationMeans: A real filter's FilterUnderTest wrapper no longer satisfies the DeviceUnderTest contract correctly — code that generically dispatches on DeviceUnderTest& (like CharacterizationRunner::run) could misbehave for real filters specifically.
entries.compares: Both real filter factories' (makeMoogFut/makeHuggettFut) kind()/excitation()/name(), accessed via upcast to the abstract DeviceUnderTest& base, against literal expected values (TransferFunction/InputSweep, and "moog"/"huggett" respectively) — proves FilterUnderTest genuinely IS-A DeviceUnderTest, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: Both real production filter models are properly registered as measurable devices in the characterization framework's type hierarchy — the upcast from the specific filter-measurement type to the generic device-measurement base genuinely works, not just in theory.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / runner accepts a DeviceUnderTest& (polymorphic)
entries.what: Passes a Moog FUT to CharacterizationRunner::run() typed strictly as DeviceUnderTest& (not FilterUnderTest&) with a 1-point grid, and asserts the resulting summary still contains the moog/LP24/fc1000/corner_hz key.
entries.why: End-to-end confirmation that the runner's public entry point works through the abstract base — this is what lets the same runner code serve filters, generators (GeneratorPathTests), and calibration devices (CaptureCalTests) without per-type overloads.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner no longer produces correct output when called through the abstract DeviceUnderTest& signature — any caller that type-erases to the base (which is most of the harness) would be affected even if concrete-type calls still work.
entries.compares: The characterization runner's output summary map, keyed "moog/LP24/fc1000/corner_hz", checked for exactly one entry, after running the FULL characterization battery through only the abstract DeviceUnderTest& base reference (not the concrete Moog type) — a structural presence check, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The entire characterization runner/battery machinery works when given only the generic DeviceUnderTest interface — any future device type (not just filters) that implements this interface could be run through the same battery without the runner needing to know its concrete type.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / ruler recovers an analytic biquad's exact response (<= 0.1 dB in-band)
entries.what: Measures an AnalyticBiquad (Q~5, fc=1000 Hz) via stepped-sine across 100 log-spaced probes 50-20kHz and asserts every measured magnitude matches the biquad's own analytic trueMagDb formula within 0.1 dB.
entries.why: An M2 trust gate: proves the ruler (stepped-sine measurement) recovers ground truth on a device whose exact answer is known by construction — tighter tolerance (0.1 dB) than the SteppedSineTests version since this is specifically the trust-gate for the measurement chain, not a method-comparison test.
entries.deviationMeans: The measurement ruler no longer recovers a known-exact analytic filter's response within the tight M2 trust tolerance — undermines confidence in every real-filter measurement built on the same ruler.
entries.compares: Stepped-sine-measured magnitude (dB) of a synthetic AnalyticBiquad (with a known closed-form trueMagDb()) at 100 log-spaced probe frequencies, against that biquad's own analytic formula, requiring worst-case error <0.1dB — an analytic closed-form comparison against a device built specifically to have a known-exact answer, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The characterization framework's core measurement tool (the 'ruler') recovers the mathematically exact response of a device whose true answer is known by construction — before ever trusting this ruler's measurement of a real, unknown filter, it's proven accurate against a case where the right answer is provably known.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / peak-gain extractor matches the analytic peak (<= 0.2 dB)
entries.what: Compares testdsp::Level::peakGainDb applied to the measured response against the biquad's true analytic peak, asserting agreement within 0.2 dB.
entries.why: Validates the peak-extraction metric (used throughout the harness, e.g. CharacterizationGateTests' peak_gain_db golden) against a known-exact peak, not just the raw response curve.
entries.deviationMeans: The peak-gain extraction logic itself has a bug — even if the underlying response curve is measured correctly, the derived 'peak' summary metric used in golden checks and reports would be wrong.
entries.compares: testdsp::Level::peakGainDb() applied to the same measured biquad response, against the biquad's own analytic true peak value, requiring agreement within 0.2dB — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared peak-gain extraction helper (used to report 'peak_gain_db' everywhere in the characterization battery) correctly finds the true peak of a known synthetic response — peak-gain numbers reported for real filters elsewhere rest on a helper proven accurate against ground truth.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / THD ruler recovers a cubic's closed-form THD (<= 0.5 dB)
entries.what: Measures THD of a pure-cubic nonlinearity (y=x+0.5x^3, which produces a pure 3rd harmonic) via testdsp::Harmonics::thdDb and compares against the closed-form trueThdDb (~-30.88 dB), asserting agreement within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: M2 trust gate for the THD measurement tool specifically (distinct from the MethodAgreementTests clean-passthrough THD-floor check) — proves it recovers an exact, non-trivial (nonzero) THD value from a device with a known closed-form answer.
entries.deviationMeans: The THD measurement tool doesn't recover a known, non-trivial distortion level accurately — any THD number reported for a real filter (e.g. the B3 distortion battery) would be suspect at the trust-gate level.
entries.compares: testdsp::Harmonics::thdDb() measured on a synthetic CubicNonlinearity (y = x + 0.5*x^3, a pure-3rd-harmonic nonlinearity with a known closed-form true THD ~-30.88dB), against that closed-form value, requiring agreement within 0.5dB — an analytic closed-form comparison, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared THD measurement tool correctly recovers the true harmonic distortion of a device built to have a mathematically known distortion amount — THD numbers reported for real nonlinear DSP stages elsewhere rest on a tool proven accurate against a provably-correct answer.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / peak / passband gain reductions over a response
entries.what: On a hand-built magnitude response { -3, 0, 6, 0, -12 } dB, asserts Level::peakGainDb returns 6.0 exactly, and passbandGainDb returns -3.0 for the Low passband and -12.0 for the High passband.
entries.why: Unit-level proof (exact, hand-computed answer) of the peak/passband extraction primitives that back headline metrics throughout the harness (e.g. RunnerLevelTests' peak_gain_db/passband_gain_db).
entries.deviationMeans: The peak or passband gain-extraction primitive is arithmetically wrong on a simple, hand-verifiable case — every derived metric using these primitives elsewhere in the suite would be suspect.
entries.compares: testdsp::Level::peakGainDb()/passbandGainDb() applied to a hand-constructed 5-point magnitude curve ({-3,0,6,0,-12}), against literal expected values (6.0 for peak, -3.0 and -12.0 for low/high passband) computed by hand from that same array, within 1e-9 — an exact arithmetic self-test, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared peak/passband-gain extraction helpers correctly identify the maximum and the edge (passband) values in a response curve — every level-based summary metric elsewhere in the suite that calls these helpers is built on arithmetic proven exactly correct.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / dBFS on a DC signal is exact (0.5 -> -6.0206 dBFS, crest 0)
entries.what: On a constant 0.5-amplitude DC buffer, asserts peakDbfs and rmsDbfs both read -6.0206 dBFS (=20*log10(0.5)) within 0.01, and crestFactorDb reads 0.0 (peak==RMS for DC) within 0.01.
entries.why: Exact-answer unit proof of the basic level primitives (peak/RMS/crest) on the simplest possible signal (DC) where peak and RMS are mathematically identical — the foundation every other level measurement in the harness builds on.
entries.deviationMeans: The most basic peak/RMS/crest-factor calculations are wrong even on a trivial DC signal — every level-based measurement anywhere in the harness (which is most of it) would be suspect.
entries.compares: testdsp::Level::peakDbfs()/rmsDbfs()/crestFactorDb() on a constant-0.5 DC signal against the exact analytic values (-6.0206 dBFS for both peak and RMS of a DC signal, 0.0 dB crest factor since peak==RMS for DC), within 0.01 — an exact mathematical identity, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared level-measurement helpers correctly compute dBFS and crest factor on the simplest possible test case (a constant signal) — every dBFS/crest-factor number reported elsewhere in the suite is built on helpers proven exact on a case with a provably known answer.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / crest factor of a sine is ~3.01 dB (sqrt(2))
entries.what: On a 1 kHz sine, asserts crestFactorDb reads 3.0103 dB (the exact peak-to-RMS ratio of a sine, sqrt(2)) within 0.2.
entries.why: Extends the DC-signal crest-factor proof to the more common (non-DC) case — a sine's exact theoretical crest factor is a well-known reference value used to sanity-check the metric on realistic audio.
entries.deviationMeans: The crest-factor calculation is wrong on a real (non-degenerate) waveform — any crest-factor-based diagnostic (e.g. MoogLadderTests' self-oscillation near-sine crest-factor check) would be unreliable.
entries.compares: testdsp::Level::crestFactorDb() on a pure sine wave against the textbook analytic constant 3.0103 dB (20*log10(sqrt(2)), the exact peak-to-RMS ratio of any sine), within 0.2 — an exact analytic identity, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared crest-factor helper correctly reads the well-known sine-wave crest factor — crest-factor claims made about real audio elsewhere in the suite are measured by a helper proven correct on the textbook reference waveform.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / noise-floor RMS recovered (uniform amp 0.1 -> amp/sqrt(3))
entries.what: On uniform-distribution white noise (amplitude 0.1), asserts rmsDbfs matches the theoretical RMS of a uniform distribution (amp/sqrt(3), ~-24.77 dBFS) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: Validates the RMS calculation against a known statistical-distribution closed form (uniform noise's RMS is analytically amp/sqrt(3)) — the noise-floor measurement building block used by RunnerNoiseFloorTests.
entries.deviationMeans: RMS measurement on noise-like (non-periodic) signals is inaccurate — the noise-floor measurement (used to catch a filter humming at idle) would be unreliable.
entries.compares: testdsp::Level::rmsDbfs() on synthetic uniform-distribution white noise (amplitude 0.1) against the analytic RMS-of-uniform-distribution formula (amp/sqrt(3), ~-24.77 dBFS), within 0.5 — an analytic statistical identity, no golden file.
entries.succeedingMeans: The shared RMS-measurement helper correctly reads the known statistical RMS of uniform noise — noise-floor measurements reported elsewhere in the characterization battery (e.g. idle-noise regression gates) rest on a helper verified against a known statistical answer.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / empty input returns the -300 dB no-data sentinel (not silence/DC)
entries.what: Asserts peakGainDb (on an empty response vector) and peakDbfs/rmsDbfs/crestFactorDb (on an empty buffer) all return exactly -300.0 within 1e-9.
